children.name: Vertical Scaling
children.notes: Adding CPU, RAM, or disk to one machine. Simple, low-coordination, but bounded by single-machine ceiling and creates a SPOF. Common starting point for early-stage Postgres or MySQL deployments. Hits hard ceilings around 1-2TB RAM and ~100 cores.

children.name: Horizontal Scaling
children.notes: Adding more machines and distributing load. Required for high-traffic systems. Introduces coordination, consistency, and partitioning complexity. Underpins all hyperscaler systems (Google, Netflix, Amazon).

children.name: Shared-Memory vs Shared-Disk vs Shared-Nothing
children.notes: DDIA Ch 2 architecture distinction. Shared-memory: one big SMP machine. Shared-disk: multiple nodes share storage (Oracle RAC, AWS Aurora). Shared-nothing: each node has its own CPU/RAM/disk and coordinates over the network (most modern distributed DBs). Only shared-nothing scales out indefinitely.

children.name: Principles for Scaling Workloads
children.notes: Identify what is actually loaded: read-heavy vs write-heavy, hot keys, peak vs sustained QPS, fanout patterns. Different workloads scale via different techniques (read replicas, sharding, caching, queues).

children.name: Availability Fundamentals
children.notes: Percentage of time system is operational and accessible. Achieved via redundancy, failover, eliminating SPOFs. Distinct from reliability (correctness).

children.name: Nines and Downtime Math
children.notes: 99% allows 3.65 days/year of downtime, 99.9% allows 8.76 hours, 99.99% allows 52.6 minutes, 99.999% allows 5.26 minutes. Each additional nine costs exponentially more. Memorize for back-of-envelope estimation.

children.name: SLA, SLO, SLI
children.notes: SLI is the measurement (actual uptime). SLO is the internal target (e.g., 99.95%). SLA is the contractual promise to customers (e.g., 99.9%) usually with penalties. Google SRE book is canonical reference. Most companies set SLO stricter than SLA to leave error budget.

children.name: Redundancy and Failover
children.notes: Active-active vs active-passive. Hot standby (immediate takeover, more cost) vs warm standby (some delay) vs cold standby (manual). RTO = recovery time objective, RPO = recovery point objective (data loss tolerance).

children.name: Reliability Fundamentals
children.notes: System performs correctly over time even when things go wrong. Includes fault tolerance, durability, consistent behaviour. DDIA distinguishes from availability: an available-but-incorrect system is unreliable.

children.name: High Availability vs Fault Tolerance
children.notes: HA = quick recovery from failure (might lose seconds of data, brief downtime). FT = continues operating with no observable disruption (more expensive: HP NonStop, RAID, redundant flight computers). Most apps want HA, not full FT.

children.name: Hardware Faults
children.notes: Disks fail (~2% annualized failure rate), RAM bit flips (especially without ECC), network cards fail. Independent failures are handled by redundancy, but at cloud scale correlated failures (rack, AZ, region outages) become a bigger threat. DDIA Ch 2.

children.name: Software Faults
children.notes: Systematic failures: bugs, memory leaks, runaway feedback loops, cascading failures. Harder to handle than hardware because correlated across nodes. Classic example: 2012 Linux leap-second bug crashing many services simultaneously.

children.name: Human Errors and Reliability
children.notes: Operator error is a leading cause of outages. Mitigations: well-designed APIs, sandboxing, automated testing, staged rollouts, easy rollback, observability, blameless post-mortems. SRE practice.

children.name: Single Point of Failure
children.notes: Component whose failure brings down the whole system. Eliminated via redundancy. Common SPOFs: load balancers, primary databases, DNS, central auth services. Identifying SPOFs is a standard interview question.

children.name: Latency vs Throughput vs Bandwidth
children.notes: Latency = time per request, throughput = requests per unit time, bandwidth = max data rate. Independent: optimizing one does not necessarily help the others. Highway analogy: latency is travel time for one car, throughput is cars per hour, bandwidth is number of lanes.

children.name: Response Time vs Latency
children.notes: Latency is the wait time. Response time = network latency + service time + queueing. Often used interchangeably but DDIA distinguishes: response time is what client sees, latency is the delay component.

children.name: Average, Median, and Percentiles (p50, p95, p99)
children.notes: Never report only averages: they hide tail behaviour. p99 = 99th percentile, meaning 1% of requests are slower. Amazon famously optimizes for p99.9 because it represents the worst experience for the highest-value customers. Latency distributions are highly right-skewed.

children.name: Tail Latency
children.notes: Latency at high percentiles (p99, p999). Tail amplification: in a service calling 100 backends in parallel, the slowest backend dominates user-perceived latency. Mitigations: hedged requests (send to multiple, take fastest), tied requests, request bypass.

children.name: OLTP vs OLAP
children.notes: OLTP = Online Transaction Processing: small interactive transactions (e-commerce, banking) on indexed rows. OLAP = Online Analytical Processing: analytical scans across millions of rows (BI, data warehouse). Different storage layouts (row vs column), different DBs (Postgres vs Snowflake/BigQuery).

children.name: Operational vs Analytical Systems
children.notes: DDIA Ch 1 framing. Operational systems run the business in real time (handling user requests). Analytical systems answer business questions over historical data. Often physically separate, with ETL or CDC pipelines moving data between them.

children.name: Systems of Record vs Derived Data
children.notes: System of record = canonical source (transactional DB). Derived data = caches, search indexes, materialized views, denormalized copies. Derived data can always be rebuilt from system of record; this principle simplifies operational reasoning.

children.name: Operability
children.notes: Make life easy for operations teams: good monitoring, automation, predictable behaviour, clear documentation, easy patching and config changes. DDIA Ch 2.

children.name: Simplicity
children.notes: Manage complexity. Abstraction is the main tool. Distinguish accidental complexity (ad-hoc patches, special cases) from essential complexity (inherent to the problem). Fred Brooks, 'No Silver Bullet'.

children.name: Evolvability
children.notes: Make change easy. Agile, TDD, refactoring, schema evolution. Systems live for years and requirements always change, so design for change.

children.name: Powers of Two
children.notes: 2^10 ~ 1KB, 2^20 ~ 1MB, 2^30 ~ 1GB, 2^40 ~ 1TB, 2^50 ~ 1PB. ASCII = 1 byte/char, Unicode UTF-8 up to 4 bytes/char. Memorize for storage estimation.

children.name: Latency Numbers Every Programmer Should Know
children.notes: Jeff Dean's famous table. L1 cache ~1ns, L2 ~10ns, RAM ~100ns, SSD random read ~100us, network round-trip same DC ~500us, HDD seek ~10ms, cross-continent round-trip ~150ms. Heuristic: memory is fast, network is slow, disk is slower. Alex Xu Ch 2.

children.name: Availability Numbers
children.notes: Common AWS SLAs: EC2 99.99%, S3 99.9%, RDS 99.95%. Multi-region composition: P(both down) = P(one down)^2 if independent. Five 9s (99.999%) is usually impossible at the application level.

children.name: QPS Estimation
children.notes: Daily active users / 86400 seconds = avg QPS. Multiply by per-user request rate, then apply peak factor (~2-5x for spikes). Reference: Twitter ~150k tweet QPS, ~600k read QPS. Alex Xu Ch 2.

children.name: Storage Estimation
children.notes: Per-record size x records/day x replication factor x retention years. Example: 500M tweets/day x 1KB x 3 replicas x 5 years ~ 2.7PB. Always include replication and retention.

children.name: Bandwidth Estimation
children.notes: QPS x payload size = bandwidth. Often surprising: 100k QPS x 10KB = 1GB/s = 8Gbps. Network is often the bottleneck before CPU.

children.name: Cache Sizing
children.notes: 80/20 rule of thumb: 20% of data gets 80% of access. Size cache for the working set, not the full dataset. Memcached and Redis instances typically sized in tens to hundreds of GB.

children.name: Number of Servers Estimation
children.notes: Total QPS / per-server QPS = server count. Add headroom (~30%) for spikes, deployments, and failures. Account for replication factor and read/write split.

children.name: Client-Server Architecture
children.notes: Client initiates requests; server responds. Foundation of every web application. DNS resolves domain to IP; HTTP carries request/response. Separating client from server lets each scale independently. Alex Xu Ch1 starts here and layers complexity on top: LB, DB replication, cache, CDN.

children.name: Monolithic Architecture
children.notes: All components (UI, business logic, data access) deployed as a single unit. Simple to develop, test, and deploy early. Pain emerges at scale: long build/test cycles, risky deploys (one bug can take down everything), inability to scale one component without scaling all. Classic first architecture before microservices or modular monolith. Alex Xu Ch1.

children.name: Microservices Architecture
children.notes: System split into independently deployable services, each owning its own data store. Benefits: independent scaling, deployment, fault isolation, polyglot stacks. Costs: network latency, distributed tracing complexity, data consistency across services, service discovery overhead. Netflix, Amazon canonical examples. DDIA Ch1, Alex Xu Ch1.

children.name: Serverless Architecture
children.notes: Functions as the unit of deployment. Cloud provider manages all infrastructure. Pay-per-invocation. Good for event-driven, bursty workloads. Cold start latency is the main tradeoff; mitigated by pre-warming or provisioned concurrency. AWS Lambda max 15-min execution, 10GB RAM. Meta XFaaS (2023) addresses stateful serverless at hyperscale. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Event-Driven Architecture
children.notes: Services communicate via events on a message broker rather than direct RPC calls. Producer emits event; one or many consumers react. Decouples temporal coupling (producer doesn't wait) and spatial coupling (producer doesn't know consumers). Challenges: ordering guarantees, at-least-once delivery, idempotency. DDIA Ch5 covers this under Dataflow Through Services.

children.name: Modular Monolith
children.notes: Single deployable binary with enforced internal module boundaries. Each module owns a business domain (Orders, Inventory, Payments) with a public API; other modules cannot access its internals directly. Shopify: Ruby on Rails monolith handling 30TB/min by enforcing module boundaries instead of microservices. Benefits: simpler ops than microservices, better than big-ball-of-mud monolith. Module boundaries can later become service cuts. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Cell-Based Architecture
children.notes: System partitioned into isolated 'cells', each a complete, independent stack serving a subset of users or tenants. Inspired by watertight compartments on ships: failure is contained to one cell. AWS, Slack, and Roblox use this. Router maps user/tenant to cell. Cells don't share databases or state. Scales by adding cells, not by making individual cells bigger. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: OSI Model
children.notes: 7 layers: Physical (bits), Data Link (frames, MAC addresses), Network (IP routing), Transport (TCP/UDP ports), Session, Presentation, Application (HTTP, DNS, TLS). Practical relevance: L3 = IP-level rate limiting (iptables), L4 = TCP/UDP load balancing, L7 = HTTP-aware LB and API gateways. Alex Xu Ch4 references layer numbers when discussing where rate limiting can be applied.

children.name: HTTP vs HTTPS
children.notes: HTTP: plaintext application-layer protocol over TCP. HTTPS = HTTP over TLS; encrypts all traffic between client and server. Without HTTPS: any network hop can read or modify data (man-in-the-middle). TLS provides: confidentiality (encryption), integrity (MAC), authentication (server certificate). HTTPS is now the baseline for any production service.

children.name: How HTTPS Works
children.notes: TLS 1.3 handshake: (1) Client Hello with supported cipher suites and random nonce. (2) Server Hello with chosen cipher, certificate signed by CA, server random. (3) Client verifies certificate against trusted CA chain. (4) Key exchange via ECDH: both sides derive same symmetric session key without transmitting it. (5) Encrypted session begins. Root CAs are pre-installed in OS/browser. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: HTTP Headers
children.notes: Categories: Caching (Cache-Control max-age/no-store, ETag, Last-Modified). Content (Accept, Content-Type, Content-Encoding). Auth (Authorization Bearer, WWW-Authenticate). CORS (Access-Control-Allow-Origin). Rate limiting (X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Retry-After from Alex Xu Ch4). Security (Strict-Transport-Security HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options). Observability (X-Request-ID, X-Correlation-ID). systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: TCP vs UDP
children.notes: TCP: reliable, ordered, connection-oriented. Three-way handshake (SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK). Retransmits lost packets. Congestion control. Used by: HTTP, databases, SSH. UDP: unreliable, unordered, connectionless. No retransmits, no handshake, lower latency. Used by: DNS, VoIP, video streaming, games. QUIC (HTTP/3) builds reliability on top of UDP. DDIA Ch9: even a TCP ACK doesn't prove the application processed the request.

children.name: TLS/SSL
children.notes: TLS 1.3 (2018) is current standard; removes insecure cipher suites, mandatory forward secrecy via ECDHE, faster 1-RTT handshake. Mutual TLS (mTLS): both client and server present certificates; used in service meshes (Istio, Linkerd) for service identity. Certificate lifecycle: ACME protocol (Let's Encrypt) for automated issuance and rotation. Certificate pinning: hardcodes expected cert in client, prevents CA compromise attacks.

children.name: Synchronous vs Asynchronous Communication
children.notes: Synchronous: caller blocks until response received. Simple, consistent, tight temporal coupling: if downstream is slow or down, caller is blocked. Used by: HTTP APIs, gRPC. Async: caller publishes message and moves on; consumer processes in its own time. Decouples availability. Adds complexity: ordering, exactly-once semantics, eventual consistency. Alex Xu Ch1 introduces message queues as the async mechanism for background jobs like photo processing.

children.name: WebSockets
children.notes: Full-duplex persistent connection established via HTTP Upgrade request. After upgrade, both client and server can push frames at any time. Used for: chat apps, live dashboards, multiplayer games, collaborative editors. Each connection is stateful, so horizontal scaling requires sticky sessions or a shared pub/sub layer (Redis Pub/Sub, Kafka) for cross-server messaging. WhatsApp and Slack use WebSockets as the transport for real-time delivery. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: WebRTC
children.notes: Browser-to-browser peer-to-peer audio, video, and data. STUN server: helps each peer discover its public IP/port behind NAT. TURN server: relay when direct P2P is blocked. Signaling (offer/answer exchange) done via your own channel (usually WebSocket). After connection: no server relay required, lowest latency path. Used by Zoom, Google Meet, Discord video. P2P doesn't scale past ~8 participants without an SFU (Selective Forwarding Unit) server.

children.name: Long Polling
children.notes: Client sends HTTP request; server holds it open until data is ready or timeout expires. On response, client immediately sends next request. Simulates server push over HTTP. Higher overhead than WebSockets: new TCP connection per response, HTTP headers each time. Used by older chat apps and systems that can't use WebSockets. Alex Xu Ch12 compares long polling to WebSockets for chat system design.

children.name: Server-Sent Events
children.notes: One-way persistent stream from server to client over HTTP using EventSource API. Server sends text/event-stream; client receives events as they arrive. Simpler than WebSockets for push-only use cases: live feeds, progress bars, notifications. Auto-reconnects with Last-Event-ID for resuming. Not all proxies/CDNs handle persistent HTTP correctly. Not suitable for bidirectional communication.

children.name: Webhooks
children.notes: Reverse HTTP: server calls your URL (HTTP POST) when an event occurs instead of you polling. You register a callback URL. Server sends event payload to it. Used by: Stripe (payment events), GitHub (push/PR events), Zapier. Reliability requires: HTTPS endpoint reachability, idempotency (events may be delivered more than once), signature verification (HMAC to prove payload is from the right sender). systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: RPC and APIs over the Wire
RPC Fundamentals
children.notes: Remote Procedure Call: make a network call look like a local function call. Abstract away network from application code. Problems RPC hides: latency, failures, retries, serialization. Leaky abstractions: RPC can't fully hide that the network is unreliable. REST is resource-oriented; RPC is operation-oriented (getUser vs GET /users/:id). DDIA Ch5 covers RPC under Dataflow Through Services. Modern RPC frameworks handle serialization, code generation, and error handling.

children.name: gRPC
children.notes: Google's open-source RPC framework. Uses HTTP/2 (multiplexing, header compression, binary framing) as transport. Protocol Buffers as serialization. Strongly typed via .proto schema. Supports: unary, client streaming, server streaming, bidirectional streaming. ~5-10x smaller payload than JSON. Used for service-to-service at Google, Netflix, Square. Code generation in 10+ languages. Tradeoff vs REST: not human-readable, harder to debug, browser support limited without gRPC-Web. DDIA Ch5.

children.name: REST vs RPC
children.notes: REST: resources as nouns, HTTP verbs as operations, stateless, cacheable, hypermedia. Advantages: universally understood, great tooling, easy to cache, browser-native. RPC: operations as verbs, tightly coupled interface, often binary. Advantages: type safety, code generation, streaming support, lower latency. Rule of thumb: REST for public APIs and browser clients, gRPC for internal service-to-service calls. DDIA Ch5.

children.name: DNS
children.notes: Domain Name System: distributed hierarchical lookup. Query flow: browser cache -> OS resolver -> recursive resolver -> root nameserver -> TLD nameserver -> authoritative nameserver -> IP. TTL controls caching at each level; low TTL enables faster failover but more DNS traffic. Key record types: A (IPv4), AAAA (IPv6), CNAME (alias), MX (mail), TXT (verification), NS (nameserver delegation). DNS is often overlooked as a SPOF; registrar lock-in and TTL values are important operational considerations. Alex Xu Ch1.

children.name: DNS Load Balancing
children.notes: Return multiple A records per domain; clients round-robin across returned IPs. No knowledge of server health: a dead server still gets traffic until TTL expires. geoDNS: return different IPs based on client's geographic location (determined by resolver IP). Alex Xu Ch1: 'users are geoDNS-routed to the closest data center.' Weighted DNS for canary or A/B traffic splits. Limitation: DNS caching means failover is slow unless TTL is very short (30-60 seconds).

children.name: Anycast Routing
children.notes: Same IP address (or prefix) announced from multiple geographic PoPs via BGP. Network routers automatically send traffic to the nearest PoP. Used by: Cloudflare (300+ PoPs), Fastly, AWS Global Accelerator. Benefits: automatic geographic load balancing, DDoS absorption (traffic distributed across PoPs), low latency. Distinct from unicast (one host) and multicast (specific group). Used for: CDN edge nodes, DNS resolvers (1.1.1.1, 8.8.8.8 are anycast), NTP.

children.name: Load Balancing
children.notes: Distributes incoming requests across a pool of backend servers. Prevents any single server from becoming a bottleneck. Provides: horizontal scalability (add servers to pool), high availability (remove unhealthy servers from pool), zero-downtime deploys (drain one server at a time). L4 LB: routes by TCP/IP headers, blind to application protocol, low latency. L7 LB: routes by HTTP headers/URL/cookies, can do content-based routing. Alex Xu Ch1: LB is added after explaining the single-server limitation.

children.name: Round Robin
children.notes: Requests distributed sequentially: server 1, server 2, server 3, server 1... Simplest algorithm. Problem: doesn't account for server heterogeneity or request heaviness. Best for: homogeneous servers, uniform request weights (e.g., stateless API). Alex Xu Ch1.

children.name: Least Connections
children.notes: Route each new request to the server with the fewest active connections. Accounts for variable request duration unlike round robin. Better for: long-lived connections, heterogeneous request weights. NGINX default for upstream groups when requests have variable processing time. Requires LB to track connection count per backend.

children.name: Weighted Round Robin
children.notes: Servers assigned integer weights proportional to their capacity. A server with weight 3 receives 3x more requests than one with weight 1. Handles heterogeneous server fleets (different hardware specs). Simple extension of round robin. Used when you want to gradually shift load (e.g., testing a new server type at weight 1 before promoting to full weight).

children.name: IP Hash
children.notes: Consistent hashing on client IP address to always route a client to the same backend. Provides sticky sessions without shared session store. Use case: WebSocket connections that must stay on one server. Problem: uneven distribution when many clients share an IP (corporate NAT). Also breaks on server pool changes. Better alternatives: cookie-based stickiness or shared session store.

children.name: Least Response Time
children.notes: Routes to the server with the lowest average response time and fewest active connections. Adapts to real-time server performance. Used by HAProxy and NGINX Plus. Most sophisticated of common algorithms; requires LB to continuously measure backend response times. Best for: heterogeneous backends or backends with variable performance. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Proxy vs Reverse Proxy
children.notes: Forward proxy: sits in front of clients. Client explicitly sends requests through it. Hides client identity from servers. Used for: corporate web filtering, VPNs, anonymization. Reverse proxy: sits in front of servers. Client doesn't know about backend topology. Handles: SSL termination, compression, caching, logging, routing. NGINX handles 1M concurrent connections on a single server via its event loop model. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: API Gateway
children.notes: Reverse proxy with application-layer intelligence: request routing, auth (validate JWT/API key), rate limiting, SSL termination, request/response transformation, caching, circuit breaking, observability. Single entry point for microservices. AWS API Gateway, Kong, Envoy, NGINX Plus, Traefik. Alex Xu Ch1: API gateway sits in front of the microservices layer. Tradeoff: single point of failure if not replicated; adds a latency hop.

children.name: API Gateway vs Load Balancer vs Reverse Proxy
children.notes: Load balancer: L4 or L7, distributes traffic by algorithm (round robin, least connections), minimal protocol awareness, no application logic. Reverse proxy: L7, caches responses, terminates TLS, hides backend topology, no application-specific logic. API gateway: superset of reverse proxy and adds auth, rate limiting, API composition (aggregate multiple upstream calls), request/response transformation, developer portal. Choose LB for raw throughput, reverse proxy for edge caching, API gateway for microservices entry point. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Caching Fundamentals
children.notes: Temporary storage of expensive-to-compute or frequently-read data. Cache hit: return from cache (fast). Cache miss: compute/fetch from source, store in cache, return. Cache tier: separate from DB, can be scaled independently. Use cache when: data is read frequently, modified infrequently, and freshness tolerance allows. Don't cache: user-specific secure data without proper isolation, or data that changes with every request. Alex Xu Ch1.

children.name: Cache Invalidation
children.notes: When cached data becomes stale after a write to the source. Strategies: TTL-based expiry (simplest, may serve stale data up to TTL), event-driven invalidation (write triggers cache delete, needs atomic DB+cache operation or CDC), write-through (update cache on every write, always consistent, higher write cost). Inconsistency window: time between DB write and cache invalidation. Alex Xu Ch1 notes consistency requires the data store and cache to be updated in the same transaction, which distributed systems make hard.

children.name: Cache-Aside
children.notes: Application code checks cache; on miss, reads from DB and populates cache. Most common pattern. Application controls cache population. On write: application writes to DB, then invalidates or updates cache (or lets TTL handle it). Risk: thundering herd on cold cache. Mitigation: mutex/lock so only one thread populates. Redis + application code is the canonical implementation. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Write-Through
children.notes: Every write goes to both cache and DB synchronously before returning to client. Cache is always in sync with DB. High write latency (two writes per operation). Read performance: always fast and consistent. Good for: read-heavy workloads where consistency is critical and write latency is acceptable. Tradeoff: unnecessary data cached (data written but never read still occupies cache memory). systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Write-Behind
children.notes: Write to cache immediately; persist to DB asynchronously in the background. Low write latency for the client. Risk: data loss if cache crashes before async write completes. Complex to implement correctly (retry logic, ordering). Use cases: write-heavy workloads where durability can be deferred (counters, analytics events, metrics aggregation). systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Read-Through
children.notes: Cache sits transparently between application and DB. On a cache miss, cache itself fetches from DB and returns to application (vs cache-aside where application fetches). Application only ever calls cache. Transparent to application code. Same thundering herd risk as cache-aside without request coalescing. Useful when you want to keep caching logic out of application code. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: LRU
children.notes: Least Recently Used. Evicts the entry not accessed for the longest time. Most common eviction policy. Redis: MAXMEMORY-POLICY allkeys-lru. Efficient O(1) implementation via doubly-linked list + hashmap. Assumption: recently accessed data is more likely to be accessed again. Limitation: doesn't account for frequency; a once-popular item evicted if not recently accessed.

children.name: LFU
children.notes: Least Frequently Used. Evicts entry with lowest access count. Better than LRU when access frequency is more predictive than recency (e.g., static reference data). Redis: allkeys-lfu. Counter decay: counters are exponentially decayed over time to prevent stale high-count items from never being evicted. More complex than LRU; slightly higher overhead.

children.name: FIFO
children.notes: First In, First Out. Evicts the oldest entry regardless of access pattern. Simple but poor hit rates because popularity is ignored. A frequently-accessed old entry gets evicted before a rarely-accessed new one. Rarely used in practice for general caches. Useful for queues and buffers where insertion order matters.

children.name: TTL
children.notes: Time-to-live: each cache entry expires after a fixed duration and is removed. Redis EXPIRE, Memcached item TTL, CDN Cache-Control max-age. Two expiry mechanisms: lazy (remove entry on next access if expired) and active sweep (background process evicts expired keys). TTL is not a consistency mechanism: stale data is served up to the TTL. Set TTL based on how often data changes and how stale your application can tolerate. Alex Xu Ch1.

children.name: Distributed Cache
children.notes: Cache spread across multiple nodes for capacity and high availability. Consistent hashing determines which node owns a key, so adding/removing nodes remaps only 1/N keys. Redis Cluster: 16384 hash slots, data sharded across nodes with replication per shard. Memcached: client-side sharding, no built-in HA. Challenge: network round-trip per cache lookup; cache nodes are SPOFs if not replicated. Alex Xu Ch5 (consistent hashing applies directly to distributed cache design).

children.name: Cache Stampede
children.notes: Multiple concurrent requests arrive for the same uncached key, all go to DB simultaneously. Also called 'thundering herd' or 'dog-pile effect'. Happens after: cache expiry, cache flush, cold start. Mitigations: mutex/semaphore (only one thread populates, others wait); probabilistic early expiry (refresh before TTL expires based on random chance); background refresh (async refresh before expiry). Alex Xu Ch6 (key-value store deep dive).

children.name: Cache Warming
children.notes: Pre-populate cache before serving live traffic. Use cases: after deploy, after cache flush, after a new region comes online. Strategies: replay recent queries, background job seeds hot keys from DB, gradual traffic ramp (canary takes traffic first, warms cache). Without warming: first traffic wave hits DB directly, causing latency spike or overload. Critical for systems with strong 80/20 access patterns.

children.name: CDN
children.notes: Content Delivery Network: globally distributed cache for static and dynamic content. Edge servers close to users serve cached content, reducing origin load and latency. How it works: client requests URL; CDN edge checks cache; on miss, fetches from origin, caches with TTL; serves from cache on subsequent requests. Alex Xu Ch1 details this flow. Key considerations: cache expiry (Cache-Control or CDN TTL), invalidation (URL versioning: image.png?v=2, or purge API), CDN fallback strategy if outage occurs. Providers: Cloudflare, Akamai, Fastly, AWS CloudFront.

children.name: Database Fundamentals
children.notes: Organized data with CRUD via DBMS. Handles concurrency, integrity, durability via transactions. Choice between relational and various non-relational types depends on data model, scale, and consistency needs.

children.name: SQL vs NoSQL
children.notes: SQL: tables, rows, schemas, ACID, joins (Postgres, MySQL, SQLite). NoSQL types: KV (Redis, DynamoDB), document (MongoDB), wide-column (Cassandra, HBase), graph (Neo4j). Most production systems use both: SQL for transactional core, NoSQL for specific scale or flexibility needs.

children.name: ACID vs BASE
children.notes: ACID = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability (relational ideal). BASE = Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency (NoSQL/distributed pragma). Tradeoff is strict guarantees vs scalability.

children.name: Relational Model
children.notes: Tables, rows, columns from Codd's 1970 paper. Joins handle relationships. Best for structured data with complex queries and many-to-many relationships. Postgres, MySQL, SQLite, Oracle.

children.name: Document Model
children.notes: Hierarchical JSON/BSON documents. Better locality (one fetch retrieves the entire document), schema flexibility, natural fit for tree-shaped data. Worse for many-to-many relationships. MongoDB, CouchDB, AWS DocumentDB.

children.name: Object-Relational Mismatch
children.notes: OOP objects vs flat relational tables: nested structures, inheritance, lists do not map cleanly. ORMs (Hibernate, ActiveRecord, SQLAlchemy) bridge with leaky abstractions. Document DBs reduce mismatch for nested data. DDIA Ch 3.

children.name: Normalization vs Denormalization
children.notes: Normalize = no duplicate data, joins assemble views (saves space, hard to read efficiently). Denormalize = duplicate for read speed, must keep copies in sync. NoSQL often forces denormalization (no joins). Tradeoff: write complexity vs read speed.

children.name: Many-to-One and Many-to-Many Relationships
children.notes: Critical for choosing between relational and document DBs. Document DBs handle one-to-many well via nesting but struggle with many-to-many (no joins). Relational handles via foreign keys and join tables. DDIA Ch 3.

children.name: Star and Snowflake Schemas
children.notes: Data warehouse modeling. Star = one large fact table at center, denormalized dimension tables around it. Snowflake = star with dimensions further normalized. Kimball methodology. Used in BI: Snowflake DB, Redshift, BigQuery.

children.name: Property Graphs
children.notes: Vertices and edges with arbitrary properties. Vertices have IDs, edges have type and direction. Better than relational for highly-connected data with arbitrary depth. Neo4j, Amazon Neptune.

children.name: Triple Stores and SPARQL
children.notes: Subject-predicate-object triples. RDF format. Used in semantic web, Wikidata, knowledge graphs. SPARQL is the SQL-like query language for triple stores.

children.name: Cypher Query Language
children.notes: Neo4j's pattern-matching query language. ASCII-art syntax for paths, e.g., (a)-[:KNOWS]->(b). Now standardized as openCypher across multiple graph DBs.

children.name: Datalog
children.notes: Older logic-based query language with recursive queries. Influenced GraphQL and modern graph DB query languages. Used in Datomic.

children.name: GraphQL
children.notes: API query language from Facebook (2015). Schema-driven, lets clients ask for exactly the fields they need. Solves over-fetching and under-fetching from REST. Not a graph DB despite the name; it is an API layer.

children.name: Log-Structured Storage
children.notes: Append-only writes, then merge or compact in background. SSTables plus memtable plus WAL. Sequential I/O is fast. Used by LSM-tree DBs (RocksDB, Cassandra, LevelDB, ScyllaDB). Write-optimized.

children.name: Page-Oriented Storage
children.notes: Mutate fixed-size pages (typically 4KB or 8KB) in place. Used by B-tree DBs (Postgres, MySQL InnoDB, SQLite). Read-optimized, predictable space usage, supports MVCC and indexes well.

children.name: Column-Oriented Storage
children.notes: Store all values for one column contiguously instead of one row contiguously. Massive compression (similar values clustered) and reads only needed columns. Used by analytical DBs: Snowflake, BigQuery, ClickHouse, Redshift. File format: Parquet, ORC. DDIA Ch 4.

children.name: In-Memory Databases
children.notes: Keep working set in RAM. Examples: Redis, Memcached (cache); SAP HANA, MemSQL/SingleStore (full DBs). Faster than disk-backed; durability via WAL or periodic snapshots. Limited by RAM cost.

children.name: Indexing Fundamentals
children.notes: Auxiliary data structure for fast lookups by some key. Tradeoff: speeds up reads, slows down writes (must update index), and consumes disk space. Choose indexes based on query patterns. Every index is a copy of the indexed columns.

children.name: B-trees and B+ trees
children.notes: Self-balancing tree, default index in Postgres CREATE INDEX. MySQL InnoDB stores primary keys as clustered B-trees. O(log n) lookups and range queries. B+ trees only store data in leaves and link them for efficient range scans. Page-oriented, in-place updates.

children.name: LSM Tree
children.notes: Log-Structured Merge tree. Memtable plus immutable SSTables on disk plus periodic compaction. Used by RocksDB, Cassandra, ScyllaDB, LevelDB, BigTable. Write-optimized: sequential writes only. Read amplification worse than B-trees due to multiple SSTable lookups.

children.name: Comparing B-Trees and LSM-Trees
children.notes: B-trees: faster reads, predictable latency, mature, in-place updates. LSM: faster writes, better compression, no fragmentation. DDIA Ch 4 says LSM is advantageous for write-heavy workloads with sequential keys, B-trees for read-heavy with random updates.

children.name: Hash Indexes
children.notes: Hashtable in memory pointing to disk offsets. Fast for equality lookups (O(1)), cannot do range queries, must fit in RAM. Used by Bitcask (Riak storage backend) and Redis.

children.name: Inverted Indexes
children.notes: Maps terms to lists of documents containing them. Powers full-text search: Elasticsearch, Lucene, Solr. The 'inverted' part: instead of doc-to-terms mapping, store terms-to-docs mapping. Postings lists, term frequency.

children.name: Multicolumn Indexes
children.notes: Index on multiple columns. Order matters: index on (a, b) helps WHERE a=? AND b=? but only partly helps WHERE b=? alone. Different from having multiple single-column indexes. Common pitfall in query tuning.

children.name: Secondary Indexes
children.notes: Index that is not on the primary key. Multiple secondary indexes per table allowed. Common in relational DBs; some NoSQL DBs (DynamoDB, Cassandra) support but with caveats around consistency or partition layout.

children.name: Clustered vs Non-Clustered Indexes
children.notes: Clustered: row data stored within the index leaves themselves (MySQL InnoDB primary key is always clustered). Non-clustered: index leaves point to row in heap (Postgres style by default). At most one clustered index per table. Affects range query performance.

children.name: Transaction Fundamentals
children.notes: Group operations into atomic logical unit. Either all commit or all abort. Foundation of ACID. Most relational DBs support multi-statement transactions; some NoSQL DBs only single-row atomicity. DDIA Ch 8.

children.name: ACID Properties in Depth
children.notes: Atomicity = all-or-nothing on commit/abort. Consistency = invariants preserved (largely the application's responsibility). Isolation = concurrent transactions do not see each other's intermediate state. Durability = committed data persists. DDIA notes the term 'ACID' is loosely defined across DBs.

children.name: Single-Object vs Multi-Object Operations
children.notes: Single-object atomicity (single row update) is widely supported even in NoSQL. Multi-object transactions (across rows or tables) are rarer in NoSQL and often missing in distributed DBs. MongoDB added them in 4.0; DynamoDB added them in 2018.

children.name: Read Uncommitted
children.notes: Weakest level. Allows dirty reads (seeing uncommitted data). Rarely used in practice.

children.name: Read Committed
children.notes: No dirty reads, no dirty writes. Default in Postgres and Oracle. Allows non-repeatable reads (the same row read twice in one transaction may differ).

children.name: Repeatable Read
children.notes: Same row read returns same value within a transaction. MySQL InnoDB default. Often implemented via snapshot isolation in practice. SQL standard's definition is weaker than what most DBs deliver under this name.

children.name: Snapshot Isolation
children.notes: Each transaction sees a consistent snapshot from its start time. Implemented via MVCC (multi-version concurrency control). Postgres SERIALIZABLE actually implements SSI on top of SI. Allows write skew, which Serializable does not.

children.name: Serializable
children.notes: Strongest level. Result equivalent to some serial execution of the transactions. Three implementations: actual serial execution (Redis, VoltDB), Two-Phase Locking (MySQL Serializable), Serializable Snapshot Isolation (Postgres).

children.name: Dirty Reads
children.notes: Reading uncommitted data from another transaction. Prevented by Read Committed and above.

children.name: Dirty Writes
children.notes: Overwriting another transaction's uncommitted write. Prevented by Read Committed and above.

children.name: Lost Updates
children.notes: Two read-modify-write cycles overlap and one transaction's update is lost. Prevented by atomic operations (UPDATE x SET v=v+1), explicit row locking (SELECT FOR UPDATE), automatic detection (Postgres SI), or compare-and-swap.

children.name: Write Skew
children.notes: Two transactions read overlapping data, decide based on what they read, then write to different rows, but each invalidates the other's premise. Classic example: doctor on-call check (both transactions see two doctors on call, both go off call). Prevented only by Serializable. DDIA Ch 8.

children.name: Phantom Reads
children.notes: Read returns different rows when run again because another transaction inserted rows matching the predicate. Predicate locks or SSI prevent. Distinct from non-repeatable reads, which are about existing rows changing.

children.name: Actual Serial Execution
children.notes: Run transactions one at a time on a single thread. Simple and fast on RAM-resident data with short transactions. Does not scale across cores. Used by Redis, VoltDB, Datomic.

children.name: Two-Phase Locking
children.notes: Acquire shared locks for reads, exclusive locks for writes. Hold all locks until commit or abort. Strong but high contention and risk of deadlock. Default in MySQL Serializable mode.

children.name: Serializable Snapshot Isolation
children.notes: Optimistic concurrency control: read from snapshot, detect serialization conflicts at commit time, abort if conflict found. Postgres SERIALIZABLE since 9.1. Lower contention than 2PL when conflicts are rare. DDIA Ch 8.

children.name: Optimistic vs Pessimistic Concurrency Control
children.notes: Optimistic = assume no conflict, validate at commit time (SSI, MVCC). Pessimistic = lock upfront (2PL). Optimistic better when contention is low, pessimistic better when contention is high.

children.name: Data Replication Fundamentals
children.notes: Keep copies of data on multiple machines. Goals: high availability, fault tolerance, lower latency (geographically near users), read scalability. Three modes: single-leader, multi-leader, leaderless.

children.name: Single-Leader Replication
children.notes: One node accepts writes and replicates to followers. Used by Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB (replica sets), Kafka (per partition). Simple consistency model. Leader is bottleneck for writes and a SPOF (mitigated by automatic failover).

children.name: Multi-Leader Replication
children.notes: Multiple leaders accept writes and replicate to each other. Used for multi-region active-active and collaborative apps (Google Docs, mobile sync). Adds conflict resolution complexity. Rare in standard DBs (BDR for Postgres, Tungsten for MySQL).

children.name: Leaderless Replication
children.notes: Any replica accepts writes, clients send to multiple replicas. Quorum-based reads/writes. Used by Dynamo, Cassandra, Riak, ScyllaDB. Tolerates node failures gracefully but anti-entropy (read repair, hinted handoff) needed for convergence.

children.name: Synchronous vs Asynchronous Replication
children.notes: Sync: leader waits for followers to confirm before acknowledging client. Strong durability, higher write latency, leader blocked if follower slow. Async: leader acks client immediately, faster but can lose recent writes on leader failure. Most setups use semi-sync (at least one follower must confirm).

children.name: Read Replicas
children.notes: Followers serve read traffic. Scales reads horizontally. Tradeoff: replication lag means reads can return stale data. Postgres streaming replication, MySQL read replicas, AWS Aurora Read Replicas.

children.name: Replication Lag
children.notes: Delay between write on leader and visibility on follower. Ranges from milliseconds to hours under load. Causes consistency anomalies: read-your-writes violation, monotonic-reads violation, causality violation. DDIA Ch 6.

children.name: Statement-Based Replication
children.notes: Replicate the SQL statement itself. Simple, but breaks for non-deterministic functions (NOW(), RAND()), auto-increment IDs, and triggers. Old MySQL default; mostly abandoned.

children.name: Write-Ahead Log Shipping
children.notes: Replicate the WAL byte-by-byte. Postgres streaming replication uses this. Tightly coupled to storage format: replicas must run the same DB version as primary.

children.name: Logical (Row-Based) Replication
children.notes: Replicate row changes (insert, update, delete) in a logical format decoupled from storage. MySQL row-based binlog, Postgres logical replication, CDC tools (Debezium). Allows version mismatches and selective replication. Foundation of CDC.

children.name: Trigger-Based Replication
children.notes: Database triggers write changes to a separate table; replication tool reads from there. Flexible (can transform during replication) but high overhead. Older tools: Bucardo, Slony for Postgres.

children.name: Last-Write-Wins
children.notes: Use timestamp to pick winner. Simple but loses data and depends on synchronized clocks. Cassandra default. Suitable when occasional data loss is acceptable.

children.name: Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs)
children.notes: Data types whose merges are commutative, associative, and idempotent. Counters, sets, maps, sequences. Used in Riak, Redis, and collaborative editing libraries (Yjs, Automerge). Enables multi-leader without manual conflict resolution.

children.name: Application-Level Conflict Resolution
children.notes: Application receives conflicting versions and decides how to merge. Examples: shopping carts (union of items), git merges. Pushes complexity up but most flexible. Riak's siblings.

children.name: Detecting Concurrent Writes
children.notes: Version vectors (vector clocks) attached to writes to detect concurrent vs causally-related operations. Different from clock-based ordering, which depends on synchronized time. Used in Dynamo and Riak.

children.name: Quorum Reads and Writes
children.notes: R + W > N guarantees reads see latest write. Tunable: lower R/W = lower latency, higher = more consistency. From the Dynamo paper. Strict quorum has edge cases like sloppy quorums and hinted handoff.

children.name: Data Partitioning Fundamentals
children.notes: Split data across nodes for scale. Each piece is a partition or shard. Goal: distribute load and storage across machines. Term 'partitioning' is used in DDIA; 'sharding' is more common in NoSQL world. DDIA Ch 7.

children.name: Sharding
children.notes: Same as partitioning, term used widely in NoSQL/MongoDB world. Each shard is effectively a smaller logical DB. Implementation: app-level (custom logic), middleware (Vitess, mongos), DB-native (Cassandra, DynamoDB).

children.name: Multitenancy
children.notes: Multiple tenants on same DB. Strategies: shared schema with tenant_id column (cheap, weak isolation), schema per tenant (medium), DB per tenant (strong isolation, expensive). Tradeoff: isolation vs operational cost.

children.name: Range Partitioning
children.notes: Partition by key range, e.g., A-D on shard 1, E-H on shard 2. Efficient for range queries. Risk: hot ranges (e.g., recent timestamps all on one shard). Used by HBase, Bigtable.

children.name: Hash Partitioning
children.notes: Hash the key, partition by hash value. Even distribution, but loses range query efficiency. Cassandra default, MongoDB hashed sharding, DynamoDB. Most common choice for KV-like workloads.

children.name: Consistent Hashing
children.notes: Hash both keys and nodes onto a ring. Adding or removing nodes only remaps a fraction of keys (~1/N). Used by Dynamo, Cassandra, memcached client libraries. Virtual nodes (vnodes) improve balance and rebalancing. Alex Xu Ch 5.

children.name: Skewed Workloads and Hot Spots
children.notes: Some keys or partitions get disproportionate load: celebrity users, viral content, recent timestamps. Mitigations: salt the hot key (append random suffix), secondary partitioning, separate cache for hot keys, application-level fanout.

children.name: Rebalancing Partitions
children.notes: Adding or removing nodes. Strategies: fixed number of partitions (Riak, Couchbase default), dynamic partitioning (HBase splits when too big), partitions proportional to nodes (Cassandra). Avoid hash-mod-N because every node-count change remaps everything. DDIA Ch 7.

children.name: Request Routing
children.notes: How does a client know which partition to query? Three approaches: any node forwards (Cassandra gossip), routing tier in front (MongoDB mongos, Vitess), client-aware (Kafka clients). ZooKeeper or etcd often track partition assignments.

children.name: Local vs Global Secondary Indexes
children.notes: Local: each partition has its own secondary index over its data; queries on the indexed value require scatter-gather across partitions. Simpler but reads are expensive. Global: the secondary index itself is partitioned (by indexed value); reads are efficient but writes update two partitions. DynamoDB GSI vs LSI.

children.name: Materialized Views
children.notes: Precomputed query results stored as a table. Refreshed on a schedule or incrementally on writes. Trade write cost for read speed. Postgres MATERIALIZED VIEW (manual refresh), Oracle, Snowflake, BigQuery (auto).

children.name: Query Optimization
children.notes: Database rewrites SQL into efficient execution plan. Cost-based optimizer uses table statistics (row counts, value distributions). EXPLAIN ANALYZE to inspect. Common wins: appropriate indexes, avoiding N+1 queries, denormalization, partition pruning.

children.name: Connection Pooling
children.notes: Reuse DB connections instead of opening per request. PgBouncer, HikariCP (Java), Django CONN_MAX_AGE. Connections are expensive: memory, auth handshake, file descriptors. Pool sizing is critical: too small causes contention, too large overwhelms the DB.

children.name: Change Data Capture
children.notes: Stream of row-level changes from a DB to downstream consumers. Powers ETL, search index sync, cache invalidation, microservice decoupling. Implementations: Debezium (Postgres logical replication, MySQL binlog), DynamoDB Streams, MongoDB Change Streams. DDIA Ch 12.

children.name: Data Warehousing
children.notes: Separate analytical DB optimized for OLAP. Loaded via ETL or CDC from operational DBs. Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Databricks. Column-oriented storage, denormalized star/snowflake schemas. Optimized for scans across millions of rows.

children.name: Key-Value Stores
children.notes: Simplest NoSQL model: hash map at scale. O(1) reads/writes by key. Redis: in-memory, supports rich data structures (strings, lists, sets, sorted sets, hashes, streams), persistence via RDB snapshots or AOF log, TTL per key, pub/sub. DynamoDB: managed, serverless scaling, single-digit-ms latency, GSI/LSI, DynamoDB Streams for CDC. Memcached: pure in-memory cache, no persistence, no rich data types, simpler than Redis. Alex Xu Ch6 designs a distributed key-value store from scratch covering consistent hashing, replication, versioning, and CAP tradeoffs.

children.name: Document Databases
children.notes: Stores JSON/BSON documents; each document is a self-contained record with nested fields. Schema-flexible: different documents in the same collection can have different fields. Good for: hierarchical/tree-structured data, content management, user profiles. Poor for: many-to-many relationships (no joins). MongoDB: most widely used, added multi-document ACID transactions in v4.0. CouchDB: revision-based, designed for sync/replication. Firestore: serverless, real-time sync for mobile. DDIA Ch3 notes document model reduces object-relational mismatch for tree-shaped data.

children.name: Wide-Column Stores
children.notes: Rows have dynamic, sparse columns grouped into column families. Efficient for: writing billions of rows with high throughput, time-series data, wide sparse tables. Cassandra: leaderless, peer-to-peer, tunable consistency (ONE/QUORUM/ALL), LSM-tree storage, linear write scalability. HBase: Hadoop-based, strong consistency, used by Facebook Messenger. Bigtable: Google's original wide-column store. Tradeoff: no joins, no ad-hoc queries, schema must be designed around access patterns. Write-optimized; Cassandra handles 1M+ writes/sec on commodity hardware.

children.name: Graph Databases
children.notes: Nodes (entities) and edges (relationships), each with properties. Relationship traversal is O(1) per hop vs O(N log N) joins in relational DBs. Ideal for: social graphs, fraud detection, recommendation engines, knowledge graphs, network topology. Neo4j: ACID-compliant, Cypher query language, property graph model. Amazon Neptune: managed, supports both Property Graph (Gremlin) and RDF (SPARQL). Tradeoff: poor for aggregations over large datasets; not a replacement for relational DB. DDIA Ch3.

children.name: Full-Text Search Engine
children.notes: Inverted index maps terms to document lists (postings). Tokenization: 'Running quickly' -> ['run', 'quick']. Scoring: TF-IDF or BM25 ranks results by relevance. Elasticsearch: distributed, REST API, near-real-time search, built on Lucene. Used alongside primary DB (CDC via Debezium or dual-write keeps search index in sync). Not a primary store: no ACID, eventual consistency. Use cases: product search, log analytics (ELK stack), text search on unstructured content.

children.name: Time Series Database
children.notes: Optimized for append-heavy writes of timestamped measurements. Schema: metric_name + tags + timestamp + value. Features: automatic downsampling (aggregate old data), retention policies (auto-delete old data), efficient compression of sequential numeric data. InfluxDB: custom TSM storage engine, Flux query language. TimescaleDB: Postgres extension, SQL-compatible. Prometheus: pull-based metrics, PromQL, 15-day default retention. Use cases: IoT sensor data, application metrics, financial tick data, system monitoring.

children.name: Vector Database
children.notes: Stores high-dimensional embeddings (e.g., 1536-float vectors from text-embedding-ada-002). Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search finds vectors closest to a query vector. Algorithms: HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs), IVF (Inverted File Index). Pinecone: managed, simple API. Weaviate, Qdrant: open-source. pgvector: Postgres extension for vector storage. Foundation of: semantic search, RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation), recommendation by similarity. Tradeoff vs exact search: ANN is approximate but orders of magnitude faster for high dimensions. systemdesign42 AI Engineering section.

children.name: Object Storage
children.notes: Flat namespace of objects (key = path, value = bytes, plus metadata). Infinitely scalable. S3: 11 nines durability via erasure coding across AZs, strong read-after-write consistency (since 2020), lifecycle rules for tiering to cheaper storage classes. Not a filesystem: no rename (copy+delete), no append (overwrite), no partial write. Multipart upload for large files. Use cases: images, videos, backups, ML training data, data lake. GCS and Azure Blob are equivalents. DDIA Ch11.

children.name: Distributed File Systems
children.notes: POSIX-like filesystem semantics spread across many machines. HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System): 128MB blocks replicated 3x, NameNode (metadata) + DataNodes (blocks), write-once-read-many semantics. GFS (Google File System): HDFS's predecessor. Used for batch processing pipelines. HDFS is weak for small files (NameNode memory bottleneck). For new systems: object storage (S3) is preferred over HDFS for most analytical workloads. DDIA Ch11.

children.name: Block vs File vs Object Storage
children.notes: Block: raw storage volumes presented to OS as disk (AWS EBS, SAN). OS manages filesystem on top. Lowest latency; databases (Postgres, MySQL) use block storage. File: network-attached shared filesystem (NFS, AWS EFS, NAS). Multiple servers mount same filesystem. Object: flat key-value HTTP store (S3). No filesystem metadata overhead; most scalable and cheapest per GB. Rule of thumb: block for databases, file for shared application state, object for large media/backups. DDIA Ch11.

children.name: Data Compression
children.notes: Reduces storage and I/O bandwidth at the cost of CPU. Algorithms: LZ4 (fastest, moderate ratio ~2x), Zstandard/zstd (good ratio ~3-4x, fast, Facebook), Snappy (Google, speed over ratio), gzip (high ratio but slow, for HTTP responses). Column-oriented storage (Parquet, ORC) benefits most: similar values in a column compress extremely well. Tradeoff: CPU cycles for compression/decompression vs I/O and storage cost savings. Usually wins on modern hardware since I/O is typically the bottleneck. DDIA Ch4.

children.name: Erasure Coding
children.notes: Encode k data chunks into n total chunks (k < n) such that any k chunks can reconstruct the original. Example: Reed-Solomon (6,3): 6 data shards + 3 parity shards; any 6 of 9 can reconstruct. More storage-efficient than full 3x replication for same fault tolerance: (6,3) uses 1.5x overhead vs 3x replication. Amazon S3 uses erasure coding internally. RAID 5/6 are erasure coding at the disk level. Tradeoff: higher CPU overhead vs simple replication. Used for cold/archival storage at hyperscale where storage cost dominates.

children.name: Language-Specific Formats
children.notes: Java Serializable, Python pickle, Ruby Marshal. Tied to a single programming language with no cross-language interoperability. Security risk: deserialization can execute arbitrary code (common CVE vector). Poor versioning support. Slow compared to binary formats like Protobuf. Never use for: data stored long-term, data shared between services, data exposed externally. Only acceptable for: short-lived in-memory caching within a single process. DDIA Ch5.

children.name: JSON, XML, and Binary Variants
children.notes: JSON: human-readable, universal browser support, native to JavaScript. Weaknesses: no distinction between integers and floats (float64 only), verbose, no schema enforcement by default. XML: more verbose than JSON, powerful schema validation (XSD). JSON Schema: widely adopted for validation and documentation; used in OpenAPI specs and schema registries (Confluent). Binary variants: MessagePack, BSON (MongoDB), CBOR: same tree structure as JSON but smaller and faster to parse. DDIA Ch5.

children.name: Protocol Buffers
children.notes: Binary encoding from Google (2008). Schema defined in .proto files; code generation in 10+ languages. Fields identified by integer field numbers (not names): enables schema evolution since old code ignores unknown fields and new code handles missing fields via defaults. Encoding: ~33 bytes for a record that JSON encodes as ~80 bytes. No field names in wire format, requires schema file to decode. gRPC uses Protobuf exclusively. LinkedIn reduced latency 60% by migrating from JSON to Protobuf. DDIA Ch5, systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Avro
children.notes: Binary encoding from Apache Hadoop ecosystem. Schema embedded in the file header (unlike Protobuf where schema is out-of-band). No field tags: field identification by position in schema. Writer's schema and reader's schema can differ; Avro resolves differences via schema evolution rules. Best for: file-based data exchange (Hadoop, data lakes) and Kafka messages with Confluent Schema Registry. Schema Registry: stores Avro schemas; Kafka messages contain schema ID, not full schema. DDIA Ch5.

children.name: Thrift
children.notes: Binary encoding from Facebook (2007), similar to Protobuf. Two binary protocols: BinaryProtocol (simple, verbose) and CompactProtocol (varint encoding, smaller). Also has JSON protocol. Multi-language code generation. Less widely adopted than Protobuf today; Protobuf's gRPC ecosystem won the service-to-service RPC market. Still used internally at Facebook/Meta and in some Apache projects. DDIA Ch5.

children.name: Schema Evolution
children.notes: Changing the schema of data over time without breaking readers or writers. Safe changes: adding optional fields (old readers ignore unknown fields), removing optional fields (new readers use default). Risky changes: changing field types (may corrupt existing data), making required fields optional (could break validation). Protocol Buffers: use field numbers, never reuse deleted numbers. Avro: changing field names breaks reader-writer schema matching. DDIA Ch5.

children.name: Forward and Backward Compatibility
children.notes: Backward compatible: new code can read old data. Forward compatible: old code can read new data. Both needed simultaneously during rolling deployments: for an interval, old and new code coexist. Achieving both: use optional fields with defaults, never remove required fields, never reuse field numbers (Protobuf). DDIA Ch5: 'rolling upgrades require all data written by new code to be readable by old code, and vice versa.'

children.name: Dataflow Through Databases
children.notes: Database as async dataflow: writer serializes data in DB, reader deserializes later. Old code must handle fields added by new code (forward compatibility). New code must handle records written by old code (backward compatibility). Schema migrations: ALTER TABLE adds column, but old rows don't have the value until backfilled. Postgres and MySQL add NULL for missing column values without rewriting rows. DDIA Ch5: 'the database acts as a data communication channel between present you and future you.'

children.name: Dataflow Through Services
children.notes: REST and RPC services evolve over time; clients and servers may run different versions. REST versioning via URI (/v1/, /v2/) or Accept header. RPC via schema evolution (Protobuf field numbers). Backward compatibility rule for services: servers must handle old requests from old clients; clients must handle old responses from old servers. API deprecation policy: maintain old versions for a defined period. DDIA Ch5 covers REST vs RPC tradeoffs in the context of service evolution.

children.name: Durable Execution and Workflows
children.notes: Long-running processes (order fulfillment, multi-step pipelines) that must complete reliably despite failures at any step. Durable execution platforms: Temporal, AWS Step Functions, Conductor. Each step is an 'activity'; failures are retried automatically. Workflow state is checkpointed so a worker crash doesn't lose progress. Temporal: open-source, code-first workflow definition. AWS Step Functions: JSON state machine definition, managed. DDIA Ch5 (2nd Ed.) introduces this as part of service-to-service dataflow patterns.

children.name: CAP Theorem
children.notes: Brewer's theorem (2000): a distributed system can provide at most two of: Consistency (every read gets the most recent write), Availability (every request gets a non-error response), Partition Tolerance (system continues operating despite network splits). Since network partitions are unavoidable in practice, the real choice is CP (sacrifice availability: refuse requests when unsure about consistency) vs AP (sacrifice consistency: return potentially stale data). DDIA Ch10 notes CAP is widely misunderstood: consistency here means linearizability, not the C in ACID.

children.name: PACELC Theorem
children.notes: Extension of CAP by Daniel Abadi (2012). Even when there is no Partition, there is still a latency vs consistency tradeoff: fast responses require giving up some consistency (e.g., reading from a local replica without checking the leader). PACELC: if Partition -> Availability or Consistency; Else -> Latency or Consistency. DynamoDB is PA/EL (availability during partitions, latency over consistency normally). ZooKeeper is PC/EC (consistency always). More practical than CAP for everyday design decisions.

children.name: Linearizability
children.notes: Strongest consistency guarantee. Every operation appears to take effect instantaneously at some point between its start and completion. Equivalent to having a single copy of the data. Required for: distributed locks, unique ID generation, leader election. Cost: requires coordination on every read and write. ZooKeeper and etcd provide linearizable reads/writes via Paxos/Raft. Single-leader DB with sync replication is linearizable. Multi-leader and leaderless are not. DDIA Ch10.

children.name: Sequential Consistency
children.notes: All nodes see operations in the same global order, but not necessarily in real-time (operations can appear to be out of real-time order as long as the order is consistent across nodes). Weaker than linearizability. Used in some parallel programming memory models (x86 TSO). Less common in distributed DB contexts. DDIA Ch10.

children.name: Causal Consistency
children.notes: Causally-related operations (A caused B) are seen in causal order by all nodes. Concurrent (unrelated) operations can be seen in different orders. Stronger than eventual consistency; weaker than sequential. Implemented via vector clocks or dependency tracking. MongoDB causal consistency sessions: ensure read-your-writes and monotonic reads across replica set members. Practical middle ground between eventual and linearizable. DDIA Ch10.

children.name: Eventual Consistency
children.notes: If no new writes occur, all replicas will eventually converge to the same value. No guarantee on when convergence happens. Cassandra default, DynamoDB default, DNS propagation. Appropriate when: temporary staleness is acceptable, availability is more important than consistency (social feeds, analytics counters). Caveat: 'eventual' can mean seconds to minutes depending on replication lag and failure conditions. DDIA Ch6.

children.name: Read-Your-Writes Consistency
children.notes: A user always sees their own writes on subsequent reads. Weaker than linearizability but often sufficient for good UX. Implementation: route user's reads to the same replica as their writes, or use leader for reads after writes, or track the write timestamp and wait until replica catches up. Prevents confusing experience of: posting a comment, refreshing, not seeing it. DDIA Ch6.

children.name: Monotonic Reads
children.notes: A user will never observe data going backward in time: if they read value V at time T, all subsequent reads return V or something more recent. Achieved by always routing a user to the same replica. Prevents the confusing case of seeing a comment, refreshing, and it disappearing. DDIA Ch6.

children.name: Network Partitions
children.notes: A network split where some nodes cannot communicate with others. Inevitable in large distributed systems: hardware failures, cable cuts, software bugs in networking stack. During a partition, nodes on each side can still communicate internally but not cross-partition. The system must decide: stop serving requests on the minority side (CP) or continue serving possibly stale data (AP). DDIA Ch9: 'the network is not reliable, this is not a theoretical concern but a practical reality in large clusters.'

children.name: Split-Brain Problem
children.notes: Two nodes simultaneously believe they are the active primary/leader and both accept writes, creating divergent state. Root cause: network partition that allows both to think the other is dead. Consequences: conflicting writes, data corruption. Prevention: fencing tokens (monotonically increasing numbers that invalidate old leaders), quorum (leader requires majority of nodes to proceed), STONITH (Shoot The Other Node In The Head: forcibly power down the other node). DDIA Ch9.

children.name: Partial Failures
children.notes: In a single machine, software fails in binary ways (process crashes or it doesn't). In a distributed system, some components fail while others continue: a partial failure. Disk I/O may fail while network is fine; one service may time out while another succeeds. This non-deterministic mixture of success and failure is the defining challenge of distributed systems. DDIA Ch9: 'a distributed system is one where the failure of a computer you didn't even know existed can render your own computer unusable.'

children.name: Unreliable Networks
children.notes: TCP guarantees delivery within a connection, but the connection itself can fail. A timeout tells you nothing: request could be lost, server could be down, or response could be lost. You cannot distinguish these cases. Packet delays can be seconds or minutes in pathological cases. DDIA Ch9: 'just because TCP acknowledged delivery doesn't mean the remote application processed the request.'

children.name: Process Pauses
children.notes: JVM garbage collection can pause a process for 0.1-30 seconds (stop-the-world GC). VM live migration pauses execution. OS scheduler preemption. During a pause, the process doesn't know time is passing and could have lost a distributed lock whose lease expired. After resuming, it acts as if the lease is still valid, creating split-brain. Mitigations: fencing tokens (any operation with a stale token is rejected by the resource), keepalive intervals shorter than lease duration. DDIA Ch9.

children.name: Byzantine Faults
children.notes: Node sends arbitrary, incorrect, or malicious messages, not just crashes or silences. Named after the Byzantine Generals Problem (Lamport, 1982). Requires 3f+1 nodes to tolerate f Byzantine faults (vs 2f+1 for crash faults). Algorithms: PBFT, Tendermint, HotStuff. Used in: blockchain consensus, aerospace systems. Not needed for most enterprise distributed systems (assume honest nodes). DDIA Ch9.

children.name: Fault Detection
children.notes: No perfect way to detect node failures in asynchronous networks. Heartbeat timeouts are the standard: if node doesn't respond within T seconds, assume failed. Timeout T is a tradeoff: too small = false positives (healthy slow nodes treated as dead, unnecessary failovers); too large = slow failure detection (users see errors longer). Phi Accrual Failure Detector (Cassandra): adaptive threshold based on historical heartbeat intervals, outputs suspicion level rather than binary dead/alive. DDIA Ch9.

children.name: Timeouts and Unbounded Delays
children.notes: In async networks, there is no theoretical upper bound on how long a message delivery can take. TCP retransmits indefinitely. OS scheduling delays can add seconds. Choosing a timeout is always a guess. Low timeout: fast failure detection, but false positives under load. High timeout: fewer false positives, but users wait longer on real failures. DDIA Ch9: 'there is no correct value for a timeout, it is a tradeoff.' Solutions: circuit breakers, hedged requests, exponential backoff with jitter.

children.name: Clock Synchronization Problem
children.notes: Different machines have different physical clocks that drift at different rates. NTP synchronizes to within ~1ms in good conditions, but can be much worse (100ms+) with network jitter. Clock can jump backward during NTP correction. Cannot use wall-clock timestamps to determine event order across machines. Google Spanner needed GPS/atomic clocks + uncertainty bounds (TrueTime) to use clocks for ordering. DDIA Ch9.

children.name: Monotonic vs Time-of-Day Clocks
children.notes: Time-of-day (wall) clock: measures wall clock time, can jump backward (NTP adjustment, DST). Never use for measuring durations or event ordering across machines. Monotonic clock: measures elapsed time since an arbitrary point, always increases, not synchronized across machines. Use for: measuring elapsed time within one process. DDIA Ch9: 'for measuring durations, always use a monotonic clock. For timestamps in distributed systems, you need TrueTime or logical clocks.'

children.name: Logical Clocks
children.notes: Track causal ordering of events without requiring synchronized physical clocks. Invariant: if A happened-before B (A causally precedes B), then timestamp(A) < timestamp(B). Two main types: Lamport timestamps (single counter) and vector clocks (one counter per node). Used when you need to determine ordering of events across nodes without relying on wall-clock time. DDIA Ch10.

children.name: Lamport Timestamps
children.notes: Algorithm: each process has a counter. Increment counter on every local event. On sending message: attach current counter. On receiving message: update counter to max(local, received) + 1. Guarantee: if A happened-before B, then L(A) < L(B). Limitation: the converse is not true. L(A) < L(B) doesn't mean A happened-before B (could be concurrent). Can't detect concurrent events. Useful for: generating unique ordered IDs in distributed systems, total-order broadcast. DDIA Ch10.

children.name: Vector Clocks
children.notes: One counter per node in a vector. Increment own counter on each event. On message send: attach entire vector. On receive: take element-wise max, then increment own counter. Allows determining: A happened-before B (A's vector <= B's on all elements), B happened-before A, or A and B are concurrent (neither vector dominates). Used in: Dynamo/Riak for conflict detection, Git commit ancestry. More expensive than Lamport: O(N) per message vs O(1). DDIA Ch10.

children.name: Heartbeats
children.notes: Periodic 'I'm alive' signals exchanged between nodes. Missing heartbeats trigger failure detection. Timeout tradeoff: too short = false positives under load; too long = slow failure detection. Gossip-based heartbeats: each node randomly sends its heartbeat to a subset of peers who propagate it further, O(log N) dissemination, no central coordinator. Used by: Cassandra (gossip), Kubernetes (kubelet liveness), etcd (election timeout). DDIA Ch9.

children.name: Hinted Handoff
children.notes: Dynamo-style availability technique. When a write's target node is temporarily unreachable, another node accepts the write on its behalf and stores a 'hint' (metadata indicating who the data really belongs to). When the target recovers, the hint holder forwards the stored write. Enables writes to succeed even when target nodes are down, at the cost of temporary inconsistency. Used by: Cassandra, Riak, DynamoDB. Part of 'sloppy quorum': writing to available nodes rather than the designated quorum. DDIA Ch6, systemdesign42 website.

children.name: Leader Election
children.notes: Designate one node to coordinate (handle writes, be authoritative). Algorithms: Raft (randomized election timeout: first node to time out sends RequestVote), Paxos (more complex, two-phase prepare/accept), Bully (highest ID wins). Key challenge: split-brain (two nodes elect themselves leader simultaneously). Prevention: quorum requirement (must win votes from majority of nodes). Fencing tokens: leader receives incrementing token; storage systems reject requests with stale tokens. DDIA Ch10.

children.name: Consensus Algorithms
children.notes: Agreement on a single value across distributed nodes despite failures. Properties: termination (all non-failing nodes decide), agreement (all nodes decide the same value), validity (decided value was proposed by some node). FLP impossibility (1985): no deterministic async consensus algorithm can guarantee termination. Solved in practice via: randomized algorithms (Raft uses randomized election timeouts), partial synchrony assumption (message delays are bounded most of the time). DDIA Ch10.

children.name: Quorum
children.notes: Minimum number of nodes that must agree for an operation to proceed. For N nodes: quorum = floor(N/2) + 1 (majority). Key property: any two quorums overlap by at least one node, so a write quorum and a read quorum always share a witness. Enables: safe leader election (new leader has at least one node from old leader's quorum, knows all committed writes), safe reads/writes in leaderless DBs. DDIA Ch10.

children.name: Paxos Algorithm
children.notes: Classic consensus algorithm by Leslie Lamport (1989, published 1998). Phase 1 (Prepare): proposer sends prepare(n) to majority; acceptors respond with promise not to accept lower-numbered proposals and any previously accepted value. Phase 2 (Accept): proposer sends accept(n, v). Notoriously difficult to implement correctly for a replicated log (Multi-Paxos). Basis for Google Chubby, Apache ZooKeeper (ZAB). DDIA Ch10.

children.name: Raft Algorithm
children.notes: Consensus designed by Ongaro and Ousterhout (2014) to be more understandable than Paxos. Three sub-problems: leader election (randomized timeouts), log replication (leader appends entries, replicates to majority, commits), safety (only nodes with up-to-date log can become leader). Used by: etcd (Kubernetes), CockroachDB, TiKV, InfluxDB Clustering, Consul. DDIA Ch10.

children.name: Gossip Protocol
children.notes: Epidemic protocol for spreading information. Each node periodically selects a random set of peers and exchanges state (membership, heartbeats, metadata). Information reaches all N nodes in O(log N) rounds. Properties: decentralized (no coordinator), fault-tolerant, scalable. Used by: Cassandra (cluster membership, schema changes), Redis Cluster, Serf, Consul. Not suitable for strong consistency; for that, use consensus algorithms. DDIA Ch10.

children.name: Service Discovery
children.notes: Mechanism for services to find each other's network addresses dynamically (addresses change with deployments, autoscaling). Two patterns: client-side (client queries service registry like Consul/Eureka, picks instance, calls directly with client-side LB); server-side (client calls LB/API gateway, LB queries registry). DNS-based: service registered as DNS name, TTL controls caching. Kubernetes: kube-dns provides DNS-based service discovery natively. systemdesign42 website.

children.name: Distributed Locks and Leases
children.notes: Lock across multiple processes/machines. Lease: lock with a TTL that expires automatically if holder crashes. Redlock (Redis): acquire lock on majority of independent Redis nodes; lock is valid if acquired on majority within timeout. Controversy: process pauses (GC, VM migration) can cause a process to hold an expired lease and act as lock holder, mitigated by fencing tokens. ZooKeeper/etcd are preferred over Redis for correctness because they provide linearizable operations. DDIA Ch9.

children.name: Coordination Services (ZooKeeper, etcd)
children.notes: Distributed key-value stores providing linearizable reads and writes, used for coordination primitives. ZooKeeper: Paxos-variant (ZAB protocol), znodes (hierarchical), watches (event notifications), ephemeral nodes (auto-deleted on client disconnect, basis for leader election). Used by: Kafka (historically), HBase, Storm. etcd: Raft-based, simpler API, v3 gRPC API. Used by: Kubernetes (all cluster state), CoreOS, Vitess. Both provide: leader election, distributed locking, config management, service discovery. DDIA Ch10.

children.name: Distributed Transactions Fundamentals
children.notes: A transaction spanning multiple nodes, services, or databases. Challenge: achieving atomicity: either all nodes commit or all abort when communication can fail at any point. Two sub-problems: atomic commitment (all agree to commit/abort) and concurrency control (isolation across distributed writes). DDIA Ch8: distributed transactions are used when a single business operation touches multiple data stores (e.g., debit account A on DB1 and credit account B on DB2).

children.name: Two-Phase Commit
