children.notes: 2PC: standard algorithm for atomic commitment across multiple nodes. Phase 1 (Prepare): coordinator sends prepare to all participants; each flushes writes to disk and responds yes/no. Phase 2 (Commit): if all said yes, coordinator writes commit to its log and sends commit to all; if any said no, sends abort. Blocking protocol: if coordinator crashes after Phase 1, participants are stuck waiting (in-doubt transactions). Coordinator's log is the commit point: recovery reads the log. Used in: XA transactions (Java EE), Google Spanner. DDIA Ch8.

children.name: Three-Phase Commit
children.notes: 3PC: non-blocking extension of 2PC. Adds a 'pre-commit' phase between prepare and commit to distinguish 'ready to commit' from 'definitely committing.' Theoretically eliminates 2PC's blocking under coordinator failure. Assumption: bounded network delays and process pauses, which async networks don't guarantee. In practice: not used because the assumption doesn't hold, and Paxos/Raft provide better alternatives for non-blocking atomic commitment. DDIA Ch8.

children.name: SAGA Pattern
children.notes: Decompose a long-running transaction into a sequence of local transactions, each with a compensating transaction for rollback. If step N fails, execute compensating transactions for steps N-1 through 1. Two coordination styles: choreography (each service listens for events and decides next step, decentralized, complex to follow); orchestration (central saga orchestrator drives each step, easier to reason about). Used by: Halo (11.6M users via Saga pattern), microservices-based order fulfillment systems. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Outbox Pattern
children.notes: Guarantee event publishing alongside a DB write without distributed transactions. Write to business table AND outbox table in the same local ACID transaction. Separate relay process reads outbox, publishes events to message broker, marks as published. If relay crashes, it re-reads unpublished outbox entries on restart: events published at-least-once. Consumer must be idempotent. Foundation of reliable event-driven microservices. Alternative to dual-write (writing to DB and broker independently, which risks partial failure).

children.name: At-Most-Once Delivery
children.notes: Send message once; do not retry on failure. Message may be lost but never duplicated. Use when: loss is acceptable and duplication is dangerous (e.g., charging a credit card: retrying a charge that was actually processed is worse than losing the charge). Implementation: fire-and-forget UDP, HTTP with no retry. DDIA Ch12.

children.name: At-Least-Once Delivery
children.notes: Retry until acknowledged. Message may be delivered multiple times. Consumer must be idempotent (processing the same message twice has the same effect as processing it once). Default in: Kafka (producers retry until acked), SQS (visibility timeout + requeue on timeout). Most message systems default to at-least-once. DDIA Ch12.

children.name: Exactly-Once Delivery
children.notes: Each message delivered and processed exactly once. Theoretically impossible in a fully async network (if ack is lost, sender retries = at-least-once). In practice: achieve via idempotent consumers + at-least-once delivery, or via distributed transactions. Kafka transactions (0.11+): producer transactions ensure exactly-once within Kafka topic-to-topic transforms. For DB writes: include message ID in write; use unique constraint to reject duplicates. DDIA Ch12.

children.name: Idempotency
children.notes: An operation is idempotent if applying it multiple times has the same effect as applying it once. Critical for at-least-once delivery systems. Natural idempotency: SET x=5 (safe to repeat), DELETE WHERE id=X (safe to repeat). Non-idempotent: INCREMENT counter (each call changes result). Idempotency key pattern: client sends unique UUID per logical operation; server stores processed keys and returns cached result on duplicate. Stripe: accepts Idempotency-Key header with 48-hour uniqueness window. API design: all POST/PATCH operations that can be retried should support idempotency keys. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Message Queues
children.notes: Durable FIFO buffer for async communication between producers and consumers. Producer enqueues message; consumer dequeues and processes; message deleted after ack. Benefits: temporal decoupling (consumer can be down, messages queue up), load leveling (burst traffic absorbed by queue). At-least-once delivery: if consumer crashes mid-processing, message is redelivered after visibility timeout. RabbitMQ: AMQP, routing via exchanges, flexible topologies. AWS SQS: managed, serverless, visibility timeout for at-least-once. Alex Xu Ch1: message queue as async job queue for photo processing.

children.name: Pub/Sub
children.notes: Publisher emits message to a topic; all current subscribers receive a copy independently. Fan-out: one publish = N consumers. Each subscriber maintains its own offset/cursor, processing one subscriber doesn't affect others. Enables: event broadcasting, decoupled notifications, multiple independent consumers for the same event stream. Google Pub/Sub: at-least-once, managed. AWS SNS: fan-out to SQS queues, Lambda, HTTP. Differs from queues: queue is competing consumers (load balance), pub/sub is fan-out (broadcast). Alex Xu Ch10 (notification system).

children.name: Log-Based Message Brokers (Kafka)
children.notes: Append-only partitioned log. Messages stored durably for configurable retention (hours to forever). Consumers track their position via offset: reading doesn't consume or delete messages. Multiple consumer groups independently read the same log at their own pace. Kafka: topics partitioned for parallelism, each partition replicated across brokers. Throughput: 150k+ writes/sec per partition, 600k+ reads/sec. Use cases: event streaming, CDC, activity tracking, audit logging, replay. Kafka Streams and ksqlDB for stream processing on top. DDIA Ch12.

children.name: Batch vs Stream Processing
children.notes: Batch: processes a bounded, finite dataset (yesterday's logs, last month's transactions). High latency (hours), simple fault tolerance (re-run failed job). Spark, Hadoop MapReduce. Stream: processes an unbounded, continuously-arriving dataset. Low latency (milliseconds to seconds), complex fault tolerance (stateful recovery, exactly-once). Flink, Kafka Streams. Lambda architecture: use both: stream for low-latency approximate results, batch for accurate historical reprocessing. DDIA Ch11 and Ch12.

children.name: ETL Pipelines
children.notes: Extract: pull data from sources (operational DBs, APIs, files) using CDC, bulk export, or API calls. Transform: clean, normalize, join, enrich (dbt models, Spark jobs). Load: write to analytical target (data warehouse, data lake). Tools: dbt (SQL-based transformations, runs in-warehouse), Airflow (pipeline orchestration), Fivetran/Airbyte (managed connectors). Modern trend: ELT (load first, transform in warehouse) vs traditional ETL (transform before loading). DDIA Ch11.

children.name: MapReduce
children.notes: Batch processing model: Map function transforms input records into key-value pairs; shuffle and sort groups pairs by key across nodes; Reduce function aggregates each key's values. Fault-tolerant: re-run failed map tasks; intermediate data written to disk (expensive). HDFS-based. Largely superseded by Spark (in-memory, DAG instead of two-phase). Conceptually important: taught the industry to think about distributed computation as data transformation pipelines. DDIA Ch11.

children.name: Dataflow Engines (Spark, Flink)
children.notes: Generalize MapReduce from two-phase (map+reduce) to arbitrary DAGs of operators (filter, join, sort, aggregate). Spark: lazy evaluation, RDDs/DataFrames, micro-batch streaming (Structured Streaming), in-memory caching for iterative algorithms (ML). Flink: true event-at-a-time streaming, stateful operators with managed state (RocksDB backend), exactly-once via checkpointing, event-time semantics. Both support batch and stream on unified APIs. DDIA Ch11.

children.name: Event Time vs Processing Time
children.notes: Processing time: wall-clock time when the event arrives at the stream processor. Event time: timestamp embedded in the event indicating when it actually occurred. They can differ by seconds to hours (network delay, buffering, mobile offline events). Processing-time windows are simple but skew results for late data. Event-time windows give accurate results but require waiting for late events. Flink uses event time by default. DDIA Ch12.

children.name: Windowing
children.notes: Aggregating stream events into finite time buckets. Tumbling window: fixed non-overlapping intervals (00:00-01:00, 01:00-02:00). Sliding window: fixed-size window that advances by a step (last 60 minutes, updated every minute, windows overlap). Session window: dynamic; groups events separated by less than a gap duration (user activity session). Windowing is necessary because you can't aggregate an infinite stream without boundaries. DDIA Ch12.

children.name: Watermarks
children.notes: A watermark at time T means: 'the stream processor has seen all events with event_time <= T.' When watermark advances past a window boundary, that window is closed and aggregated. Tradeoff: aggressive watermark (low lag tolerance) closes windows quickly but may drop late events. Conservative watermark (high lag tolerance) waits longer for late events but increases output latency. Flink: configurable watermark strategies. DDIA Ch12.

children.name: Stream Joins
children.notes: Join streams or streams with tables. Stream-stream join: both inputs time-windowed; events in the same window period are joined. Stream-table join (enrichment): enrich each stream event with current value from a DB table (e.g., add user profile to click event). Table-table join (materialized view): maintain a running join as both tables receive CDC events. Implementation: requires partitioning both inputs by the join key so matching records land on the same processor. DDIA Ch12.

children.name: API Design Principles
children.notes: Good APIs are consistent (same patterns throughout), minimal (expose what's needed, nothing more), evolvable (backward compatible changes), and intuitive (follows expected conventions). HTTP: use nouns for resources not verbs, standard status codes (200/201/400/404/429/500), meaningful error bodies (machine-readable error code + human-readable message). Versioning strategy from day one. Document with OpenAPI/Swagger. Never break backward compatibility in a released version. Alex Xu throughout; systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: REST APIs
children.notes: Representational State Transfer (Fielding, 2000). Constraints: uniform interface (resources identified by URLs, HTTP verbs as operations), stateless (each request self-contained, no server-side session), cacheable (responses declare cacheability via headers), layered (client doesn't know if talking to origin or proxy). HTTP verbs: GET (read, idempotent), POST (create), PUT (replace, idempotent), PATCH (partial update), DELETE (idempotent). Status codes: 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 429 Too Many Requests, 500 Internal Server Error. DDIA Ch5.

children.name: Rate Limiting
children.notes: Control request rate to prevent abuse and protect downstream systems. Algorithms: token bucket (bucket fills at rate R, each request consumes a token, allows bursts up to bucket size), leaking bucket (fixed output rate, queue incoming, smooths output), fixed window counter (count per time window, has edge burst problem), sliding window log (timestamp-based, precise), sliding window counter (hybrid, approximate). Alex Xu Ch4: Redis counter store (INCR + EXPIRE), centralized Redis for multi-server sync. Headers: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Retry-After. HTTP 429 Too Many Requests.

children.name: API Versioning
children.notes: Strategies: URI versioning (/v1/users, /v2/users), most visible, easy to route, cache-friendly; Header versioning (Accept: application/vnd.api.v2+json), cleaner URLs, less visible; Query parameter (?version=2), simple but pollutes URLs. URI versioning is most widely used (Stripe, Twilio). Never remove or change semantics of existing endpoints in a released version; only add. Deprecation lifecycle: announce, provide migration path, sunset date. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Idempotent APIs
children.notes: An API is idempotent if the same request can be safely retried without changing the result beyond the first call. GET, PUT, DELETE are naturally idempotent. POST is not (creates new resource each call). Idempotency key pattern: client generates a UUID and sends it in a header (Idempotency-Key) or body. Server stores the key + result in a DB (with unique constraint); on duplicate key, return stored result without re-executing. Stripe: 48-hour idempotency window. Critical for: payment APIs, any mutation that could be retried due to timeout. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Pagination (Cursor vs Offset vs Page Number)
children.notes: Three strategies for paginating large result sets. Offset pagination: LIMIT N OFFSET M — simple SQL, but slow at large offsets (DB must scan M rows to skip them) and inconsistent if rows are inserted/deleted mid-session. Page number: same issue as offset, just expressed differently. Cursor pagination: uses an opaque pointer (last seen ID or encoded timestamp) as the WHERE clause anchor (WHERE id > :cursor LIMIT N). O(1) regardless of page depth, stable across mutations. Use cursor for infinite scroll (Twitter, Instagram), offset for admin tables with page numbers. Trade-off: cursor pagination can't jump to arbitrary pages.

children.name: GraphQL
children.notes: API query language from Facebook (2015). Clients specify exactly the fields they need in the request — no over-fetching (unused fields) or under-fetching (needing a second request). Single endpoint (/graphql), operations: query (read), mutation (write), subscription (real-time via WebSocket). Schema-first: strongly typed SDL defines all types and operations. N+1 problem: naively resolving nested fields makes N queries for N objects; solved by DataLoader (batching + caching per request). Introspection: clients can discover the schema. Used by: GitHub API, Shopify Storefront API, Twitter API v2. Tradeoff vs REST: no HTTP-level caching (POST body is unique per query), more complex server setup. DDIA Ch5 context; systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Authentication vs Authorization
children.notes: Authentication (AuthN): verify identity: 'who are you?' (username+password, OAuth token, mTLS certificate). Authorization (AuthZ): verify permission: 'what are you allowed to do?' (RBAC roles, ACLs, policy engines). AuthN always happens before AuthZ: you must know who someone is before deciding what they can do. Common mistake: conflating them in middleware. OAuth2 is an AuthZ framework (grant access to resources). OpenID Connect adds AuthN on top of OAuth2 (issue identity tokens). JWT often carries both identity claims and permission scopes.

children.name: Session-based vs Token-based Authentication
children.notes: Session: server creates session on login, stores in Redis/DB (session ID to user data mapping), returns session cookie. Stateful: requires shared session store for horizontal scaling. Token (JWT): server issues self-contained signed token. Client sends token with each request; server validates signature without DB lookup. Stateless: scales horizontally. Tradeoff: tokens can't be revoked until expiry without a blocklist, which reintroduces state. Short-lived access tokens + long-lived refresh tokens is the standard pattern. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: OAuth, OAuth2, OpenID Connect
children.notes: OAuth2: authorization framework. Allows user to grant third-party app access to their resources without sharing credentials. Core flows: Authorization Code (browser apps, code exchanged for token server-side, most secure), Client Credentials (service-to-service, no user involved), Device Code (TV/CLI, user authorizes on second device). Tokens: access token (short-lived, API calls), refresh token (long-lived, get new access token). PKCE prevents authorization code interception. OpenID Connect (OIDC): extends OAuth2 with ID token (JWT with user claims: sub, email, name). systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: JWT
children.notes: JSON Web Token: three Base64url-encoded parts separated by dots: Header (algorithm, type), Payload (claims), Signature. Signature: HMAC-SHA256 with secret (symmetric) or RS256 with RSA private/public key pair (asymmetric: issuer signs, validators only need public key). Standard claims: sub (subject/user ID), iss (issuer), exp (expiry Unix timestamp), iat (issued at), aud (audience). Stateless: no DB lookup on verification. JWTs can't be revoked mid-lifetime; mitigate with short exp (15 min) + refresh token. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Single Sign-On
children.notes: One login authenticates the user across multiple applications. Identity Provider (IdP) performs authentication and issues tokens/assertions; Service Providers (SPs) trust the IdP and accept its assertions. Protocols: SAML 2.0 (XML-based, enterprise, browser-based SSO via POST bindings: Okta, ADFS), OpenID Connect/OAuth2 (JSON-based, modern: Google, Okta, Auth0). Session management: SP-initiated vs IdP-initiated SSO. Enterprise use: employees log into Okta once, access Salesforce, Slack, GitHub without re-authenticating. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Role-Based Access Control
children.notes: Permissions assigned to roles; users and service accounts assigned to roles. Decouples permission management from individual users. Example: roles = Admin, Editor, Viewer; users assigned to roles; role defines which API endpoints/DB rows/S3 buckets are accessible. Principle of least privilege: assign only the permissions needed. ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control): permissions based on attributes (user department, resource sensitivity, time of day), more flexible but more complex to manage. OPA (Open Policy Agent): policy engine for fine-grained authorization. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Security Secrets Management
children.notes: Secrets (API keys, DB passwords, TLS certs) must never be committed to version control or stored in plaintext env vars. Secrets manager: HashiCorp Vault (open-source, dynamic secrets: generates short-lived DB credentials on demand), AWS Secrets Manager (managed, rotation built-in), GCP Secret Manager. Dynamic secrets: Vault creates a short-lived Postgres user for each service instance so no shared long-lived credential. Secret rotation: change secrets on a schedule or on suspected compromise. Service mesh (Istio) handles mTLS cert rotation automatically. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: API Security Best Practices
children.notes: Authentication on every endpoint (no anonymous access to sensitive data). HTTPS only: no HTTP plaintext. Input validation and parameterized queries (prevent SQL injection, XSS). Rate limiting (prevent brute force and scraping). No sensitive data in query params (logged in access logs). Error messages: return generic errors to client, log details server-side. CORS: restrict Access-Control-Allow-Origin to known domains. OWASP API Security Top 10: broken object-level auth, excessive data exposure, improper inventory management. Validate JWT signature and exp claim. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: How Databases Store Passwords
children.notes: Never store plaintext passwords. Hashing: one-way function, can't reverse. MD5 and SHA-1: deprecated for passwords (fast = attackable via GPU brute force, rainbow tables). Salt: random unique value prepended to password before hashing, prevents rainbow table attacks and identical passwords from producing identical hashes. bcrypt: intentionally slow (configurable cost factor), built-in salt. scrypt and Argon2: memory-hard (require GB of RAM per hash, prevents GPU/ASIC attacks). Argon2 won the Password Hashing Competition (2015) and is preferred for new systems. Timing-safe comparison to prevent timing attacks. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Circuit Breaker Pattern
children.notes: Prevent cascading failures when a downstream service is degraded. States: Closed (normal, requests pass through, failure rate tracked), Open (downstream assumed failed, requests fail fast without calling downstream), Half-Open (after cooldown period, allow one probe request: if it succeeds, close; if it fails, reopen). Failure rate thresholds and window sizes are configurable. Implementations: Netflix Hystrix, resilience4j (Java), Polly (.NET), Envoy/Istio (service mesh). Named after electrical circuit breakers: open = no current flows.

children.name: Bulkhead Pattern
children.notes: Isolate failures to a partition of the system so one slow dependency can't starve all others. Named after ship bulkhead compartments (watertight walls preventing flooding from spreading). Implementation: separate thread pools or connection pools per dependency. If the Payments service's thread pool is exhausted, requests to Search are unaffected. Resource isolation: separate CPU quotas, memory limits, connection limits per service or tenant. Kubernetes resource requests/limits per container implement bulkheads at the OS level.

children.name: Strangler Fig Pattern
children.notes: Incremental monolith-to-microservices migration. New functionality built as separate services from day one. Old functionality gradually extracted from monolith into new services. A facade (API gateway, proxy) routes requests to either old monolith or new services. Eventually the monolith is completely replaced and can be decommissioned. Named after the strangler fig tree (Ficus aurea) that grows around a host tree, eventually replacing it. Avoids risky big-bang rewrites. Used by Shopify, Booking.com, Amazon.

children.name: Backend for Frontend
children.notes: Dedicated API aggregation layer per client type. Mobile BFF: aggregates multiple downstream calls, returns mobile-optimized payload (smaller, fewer fields). Web BFF: different data shape for web. External API BFF: public API contract. Benefits: reduces over-fetching (mobile doesn't need all fields), reduces under-fetching (web gets aggregated data in one call), each BFF can evolve independently. Pattern introduced by Sam Newman in 2015. Downside: code duplication across BFFs, mitigated with shared libraries for common logic.

children.name: Sidecar Pattern
children.notes: Deploy a helper container alongside the main service container in the same Kubernetes pod. They share the same network (localhost) and volume mounts. Sidecar handles cross-cutting concerns without changing application code: TLS termination, mTLS to other services, Prometheus metrics exposition, log shipping (Fluentd), distributed tracing (Jaeger agent). Service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) injects Envoy sidecar proxies automatically into every pod. Downside: resource overhead per pod and operational complexity. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Service Mesh
children.notes: Infrastructure layer for service-to-service communication, implemented via sidecar proxies (Envoy) automatically injected into pods. Provides: mTLS (automatic cert management and rotation), distributed tracing (auto-instrument spans), circuit breaking, retries, timeouts, load balancing, all without application code changes. Control plane: Istio pilot (configuration), Citadel (certs). Data plane: Envoy proxies. Cost: 10-30% CPU overhead per service and operational complexity. Linkerd is a simpler, lighter alternative. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Chaos Engineering
children.notes: Discipline of intentionally injecting failures into production systems to discover weaknesses before they cause customer-facing incidents. Netflix Chaos Monkey (2011): randomly terminates EC2 instances in production during business hours. Principles: define steady state (measurable healthy baseline), hypothesize steady state holds under failure, inject failure (random instance kill, network latency, disk full), observe and act if hypothesis fails. Tools: Chaos Monkey, Gremlin, AWS Fault Injection Simulator. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Observability Fundamentals
children.notes: Ability to understand internal system state from external outputs. Three pillars: Logs (discrete events with context), Metrics (aggregated numeric measurements over time), Traces (end-to-end request flow across services). Observability vs monitoring: monitoring tells you when something is wrong; observability lets you understand why (ask previously unasked questions about system state). OpenTelemetry: open standard for logs, metrics, and traces in a unified API.

children.name: Logging
children.notes: Record of discrete events. Structured logging (JSON with consistent fields: timestamp, level, service, trace_id, message) vs unstructured text (harder to parse programmatically). Log levels: DEBUG (verbose, dev only), INFO (normal operation events), WARN (unexpected but recoverable), ERROR (action failed, needs investigation). Centralized logging: ship logs to Elasticsearch (ELK stack), Datadog, CloudWatch, Loki. Correlation IDs in logs enable tracing a request across services. Alex Xu Ch1: 'monitor error logs at per-server level or aggregate to a centralized service for easy search.'

children.name: Metrics
children.notes: Numeric time-series measurements. Types: Counter (cumulative, monotonically increasing: total requests), Gauge (current value at a point in time: active connections, queue depth), Histogram (distribution of values: request latency percentiles). RED method: Rate (requests/sec), Errors (error rate), Duration (latency). USE method: Utilization, Saturation, Errors. Alex Xu Ch1 three tiers: host-level (CPU/memory/disk I/O), service-level (request rate/error rate/latency), business-level (DAU/revenue). Prometheus scrapes metrics; Grafana visualizes. Datadog, CloudWatch as managed alternatives.

children.name: Distributed Tracing
children.notes: Track a single request as it flows through multiple services. Each service creates a span (start time, end time, service name, operation name, tags, status). Spans within a trace share a trace ID (propagated in request headers: W3C Trace Context standard or B3). Parent-child relationships form the trace tree. Reveals: which service is slow, which downstream calls are made, where errors originate. Sampling: full sampling is too expensive; head-based (~1%) or tail-based (sample traces with errors/high latency). OpenTelemetry, Jaeger, Zipkin, Datadog APM. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Correlation IDs
children.notes: Unique identifier generated at the entry point of a request (API gateway, LB) and propagated through all downstream service calls via HTTP headers. All log lines for a request include the correlation ID, enabling grep/search to reconstruct the full request journey across services. Header names: X-Request-ID (single service context) or X-Correlation-ID / X-Trace-ID (cross-service). Also included in error responses to clients; support can look up the exact request in logs. Essential for debugging production issues in microservices. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: CQRS
children.notes: Command Query Responsibility Segregation: separate the write model (Commands: validate business rules, update state, emit domain events) from the read model (Queries: return data in the shape the client needs). Write model: normalized, enforces invariants, owns the source of truth. Read model: denormalized projections optimized for specific queries (e.g., precomputed news feed). Projections rebuilt by replaying events. Benefits: read and write models scale independently; read models can be tuned per use case. Natural fit with Event Sourcing. Complexity: two models to maintain, eventual consistency between write and read. DDIA Ch3 (2nd Ed.).

children.name: Event Sourcing
children.notes: Instead of storing current state, store an immutable append-only log of all state-changing events. Current state derived by replaying events from the beginning (or from a snapshot + subsequent events). Benefits: complete audit trail, ability to replay events to rebuild projections or fix bugs, temporal queries ('what was the state at T?'). Challenges: event schema evolution (old events must still be processable), snapshot management for performance, eventual consistency of read projections. DDIA Ch3 (2nd Ed.): event sourcing is a data modeling approach related to immutable logs and derived data. Used by: Axon Framework, Kafka-based event stores.

children.name: Checksums
children.notes: A checksum is a small fixed-size value derived from data, used to detect accidental corruption. Not for security (use MAC/HMAC for integrity with authentication). CRC32: fast hardware-accelerated, used by filesystems (ext4, ZFS) and network protocols. MD5: broken for cryptographic security but still used for quick integrity checks (S3 object ETags). SHA-256: cryptographically secure, used by Git (content-addressed storage) and TLS. HDFS: checksums each block on write, verifies on read. Corruption detection: if stored checksum != computed checksum -> data corruption detected -> fetch from replica. systemdesign42 website.

children.name: Bloom Filter
children.notes: Probabilistic set membership data structure. Extremely space-efficient: represents a set of N items in M bits. Operations: insert(x): hash x with k hash functions, set k bits; contains(x): check if all k bits are set. False positives possible (might say 'present' for items not in the set). False negatives impossible (if all bits not set, definitely absent). False positive rate tunable: more bits = lower FPR. Use cases: Cassandra (check if SSTable might contain a key before disk read, reduces I/O), Chrome Safe Browsing, Postgres planner, CDN duplicate URL detection. systemdesign42 website.

children.name: Quotient Filter
children.notes: Alternative to Bloom Filter with better cache performance. Stores a compact fingerprint of each element in a contiguous array (better cache locality than Bloom's multiple scattered bit arrays). Supports: merge of two filters, resize without rehashing (can grow dynamically). Slightly higher false positive rate for same memory vs Bloom. Better for: dynamic workloads that need resizing, systems where cache efficiency matters. Less widely implemented than Bloom in practice. systemdesign42 website.

children.name: Merkle Tree
children.notes: Binary tree where each leaf node contains the hash of a data block, and each internal node contains the hash of its children. Root hash represents the entire dataset. Properties: tamper-evident (changing one block changes all ancestor hashes up to root), efficient comparison (compare root hashes first, recurse only into differing subtrees). Use cases: Git (each commit is a Merkle tree of the file tree, content-addressed, deduplication), Bitcoin (transactions in a block form a Merkle tree), Cassandra/DynamoDB anti-entropy (compare Merkle trees of partition data to find diverged replicas without full data transfer). systemdesign42 website.

children.name: HyperLogLog
children.notes: Probabilistic algorithm for estimating the count of distinct elements in a stream. Uses only ~1.5KB of memory regardless of input size with ~2% standard error. Algorithm: hash each element, observe leading zeros in binary hash, keep maximum across many sub-estimators, combine via harmonic mean. Redis: HyperLogLog is a first-class type (PFADD to add element, PFCOUNT for cardinality, PFMERGE to union). Use cases: counting unique visitors, distinct IP addresses, unique search queries in analytics pipelines. When exact count not needed and memory matters. systemdesign42 website.

children.name: Deployment Patterns
children.notes: Strategies for delivering new code to production safely. Key concerns: minimize downtime, limit blast radius, enable fast rollback. Rolling: replace instances one at a time; some users get old version, some get new version simultaneously. Blue-Green: two identical environments; switch traffic all at once; instant rollback. Canary: route small traffic percentage (1-5%) to new version; monitor; promote or rollback. Feature flags: ship code behind an off switch; enable gradually. Shadow deployment: send copy of production traffic to new version without serving responses. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Blue-Green Deployment
children.notes: Maintain two identical production environments: Blue (current live) and Green (new version). Deploy new version to Green; run smoke tests. Switch all traffic at LB/DNS level from Blue to Green. Instant rollback: switch back to Blue. Benefits: zero-downtime deployment, immediate rollback capability. Costs: 2x infrastructure (both environments must be production-sized), data migration complexity (both environments share DB or need sync). Used by: AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Spinnaker. Best for stateless services; databases complicate blue-green since schema migrations must be backward compatible with both versions. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Canary Deployment
children.notes: Route a small percentage of production traffic (1-5%) to the new version while the majority continues to the old version. Monitor error rate, latency, and business metrics on the canary. If canary is healthy after a validation period, gradually increase to 25%, 50%, 100%. If metrics degrade, immediately roll back the canary percentage to 0%. Named after the mining practice of bringing a canary into coal mines to detect toxic gases. Tools: Spinnaker, Argo Rollouts, Flagger (Kubernetes), AWS CodeDeploy. Netflix uses sophisticated canary analysis with automated promotion/rollback. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Feature Flags
children.notes: Toggle features on/off in production without deploying new code. Stored in a config service (LaunchDarkly, Unleash, Statsig) evaluated at runtime. Use cases: gradual rollout (enable for 1% of users, then 10%, then 100%), A/B testing (random 50/50 split), kill switch (disable a broken feature instantly), permission gates (enable only for beta users). Decouple deployment from release: ship code anytime, control feature visibility separately. Risk: flag debt: old flags never cleaned up become code complexity. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Infrastructure as Code
children.notes: Define and provision infrastructure via code, stored in version control. Declarative: describe desired state; tool handles creating/updating/destroying resources (Terraform, AWS CDK, Pulumi). Imperative: describe steps to reach desired state (Ansible playbooks). Terraform: cloud-agnostic, provider plugins for AWS/GCP/Azure, plan (dry run) + apply workflow, state file tracks what's deployed. Benefits: reproducibility (identical environments from same code), PR review for infra changes, rollback via git revert. DDIA Ch2: 'operability: automation is a key property of maintainable systems.'

children.name: Concurrency vs Parallelism
children.notes: Concurrency: dealing with multiple tasks simultaneously by interleaving execution (a single-core CPU switching between tasks). Structure, not execution. Parallelism: physically executing multiple tasks at the same time on multiple CPU cores. Execution, not structure. A concurrent design does not imply parallel execution (may run on single core). Rob Pike (Go creator): 'Concurrency is not parallelism: concurrency is about structure, parallelism is about execution.' Go's goroutines: concurrent by design, parallel if GOMAXPROCS > 1. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Actor Model
children.notes: Concurrency model where actors are the fundamental unit. Each actor has: private state (no shared memory), a mailbox (message queue), and behavior (processes one message at a time). Actors communicate only via async message passing: no shared mutable state = no race conditions, no locks. On receiving a message, an actor can: update its state, send messages to other actors, create new actors. Erlang/Elixir: actors = processes, lightweight, millions per node. Akka (JVM). PayPal: 1 billion transactions/day on 8 VMs using Akka actor model. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Thread-Based Concurrency
children.notes: OS threads scheduled by the kernel. Each thread has its own stack (1-8MB). Synchronization via: mutexes (mutual exclusion locks), semaphores, condition variables. Problems: race conditions (unsynchronized access to shared state), deadlocks (circular lock acquisition), context switch overhead (~1-10 microseconds). Apache HTTPd: thread-per-request model, one thread per connection. Limits: high memory overhead at scale (10k connections x 2MB stack = 20GB RAM). Java virtual threads (JDK 21, Project Loom): lightweight threads mapped M:N to OS threads, millions of virtual threads without the overhead.

children.name: Event Loop Model
children.notes: Single-threaded event loop dispatches I/O events to registered handlers. Non-blocking I/O: instead of blocking for DB response, register callback and process other events while waiting. When I/O completes, callback is queued. Never blocks the event loop on I/O or CPU-heavy work. Node.js: JavaScript event loop + libuv for async I/O. NGINX: event-driven, handles 1M+ concurrent connections on one server via non-blocking epoll (Linux) vs Apache's thread-per-connection model. Redis: single-threaded event loop for command processing, no lock contention. Weakness: CPU-bound tasks block the event loop; use worker threads for CPU work. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: LLM Concepts
children.notes: Large Language Models: neural networks trained to predict the next token given a context. Key concepts: context window (max tokens the model can process in one call: GPT-4 128k, Claude 200k); temperature (0 = deterministic/greedy; 1+ = more random, creative); top-p / top-k (nucleus/top-k sampling limits the token pool); tokens (roughly 3-4 characters; pricing is per token). Not a knowledge database: it is a probability model over token sequences. Hallucination: confident generation of plausible but false content. document2.pdf (goflash): 'LLM predicts next token based on training patterns, not a knowledge database.' systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
children.notes: Reduce hallucinations and inject current knowledge by retrieving relevant documents at query time and including them in the prompt. Pipeline: (1) Offline: chunk documents, embed with embedding model (text-embedding-3-small), store vectors in vector DB. (2) Online: embed user query, similarity search in vector DB (HNSW/ANN), retrieve top-K chunks, inject chunks into prompt as context, LLM generates answer grounded in retrieved context. Tradeoffs: retrieval quality determines answer quality; chunk size and overlap affect context coherence; re-ranking (cross-encoder) improves relevance at extra cost. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: AI Agents
children.notes: LLM given tools (web search, code execution, API calls, file read/write) and instructions to complete multi-step tasks autonomously. ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) loop: LLM reasons about the task, calls a tool (Action), observes the result, reasons again. Challenges: long context degrades quality; tool call hallucinations; multi-step error accumulation; cost (each step is an API call). Two agent patterns: single-agent (one LLM with many tools), multi-agent (specialized LLMs calling each other). Memory: in-context (retrieved each turn), external (vector DB for long-term). systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Model Context Protocol (MCP)
children.notes: Open standard by Anthropic (2024) for connecting LLMs to external tools and data sources. Architecture: MCP hosts (Claude Desktop, IDEs) connect to MCP servers via JSON-RPC over stdio or HTTP/SSE. MCP servers expose: tools (callable functions), resources (readable data), prompts (template strings). Enables: composable AI integrations without bespoke plugin code per tool. Any MCP server works with any MCP-compatible host. Growing ecosystem: GitHub, Google Drive, Slack, Postgres all have MCP servers. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Context Engineering
children.notes: The discipline of designing the full information environment the model sees: system prompt, conversation history, retrieved documents, tool results, memory. Karpathy: 'the delicate art and science of filling the context window with just the right information for the next step.' Key principles: relevant > comprehensive (cutting noise improves quality), structured context (JSON/XML with clear labels), recency bias (recent context more influential), token budget management. Distinct from prompt engineering: context engineering manages the entire state, not just the wording. systemdesign42 newsletter, document2.pdf.

children.name: Context Engineering vs Prompt Engineering
children.notes: Prompt engineering: improving individual prompts, word choice, few-shot examples, chain-of-thought instructions. Context engineering: designing the entire information environment across a session: what goes in the system prompt, how memory is retrieved, how tool results are formatted, how conversation history is managed. Prompt engineering is a subset of context engineering. For simple single-turn tasks, prompt engineering is enough. For complex multi-turn agentic systems (like production AI assistants), context engineering is the critical discipline. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Reinforcement Learning
children.notes: Agent learns by taking actions in an environment and receiving reward signals. Key components: state (current situation), action (choice the agent makes), reward (feedback signal), policy (mapping from state to action). RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback): humans rank model outputs; a reward model is trained on rankings; the LLM is fine-tuned to maximize the reward model's scores via PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization). Used to align GPT-4, Claude, Gemini with human preferences. DPO (Direct Preference Optimization): simpler alternative that bypasses the reward model. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: AI Coding Workflows
children.notes: Patterns for using AI models (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude Code) to accelerate software development. Key patterns: inline completion (autocomplete as you type), chat-driven development (describe what you want, get code), test-first generation (write test, ask AI to implement), iterative refinement (start broad, refine in turns). document2.pdf (goflash): Claude used to design the app architecture; DeepSeek used to generate flashcard content. Key insight from document.pdf: structured prompts with explicit output format (JSON only, no preamble) + temperature near 0 give reliable structured output. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Amazon Dynamo Architecture
children.notes: 2007 paper: Amazon's highly available KV store for shopping cart (70% of Amazon operations needed only simple KV model). Techniques combined: consistent hashing (ring with virtual nodes for even distribution and minimal remapping), quorum reads/writes (R+W>N, tunable consistency), vector clocks (detect concurrent writes for conflict resolution), hinted handoff (write to healthy node when target is down, deliver on recovery), gossip protocol (membership and failure detection without central coordinator), Merkle trees (anti-entropy: compare hash trees to find diverged data efficiently). AP system: chose availability over consistency. DDIA Ch6 is heavily influenced by this paper. Foundation of Cassandra, Riak, DynamoDB. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Google Spanner
children.notes: Google's globally distributed relational database. Novel contribution: TrueTime: GPS receivers and atomic clocks at every datacenter provide timestamps with guaranteed bounded uncertainty (plus or minus 7ms). Spanner exposes TrueTime API: TT.now() returns [earliest, latest] interval. Transactions: commit waits until TT.now().earliest > commit timestamp, guarantees linearizability across globally distributed transactions. Architecture: zones (physical deployments), Paxos groups per shard, tablets for data partitioning. Supports: SQL, multi-row ACID transactions, globally consistent reads at a timestamp. GCP Cloud Spanner is the managed version. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Meta XFaaS (Serverless Architecture)
children.notes: Meta's stateless and stateful Function-as-a-Service platform (2023 paper). Problem: cold starts in traditional serverless add hundreds of ms of latency for ML inference and event processing. Solutions: worker pre-warming (keep warm worker pool ready, assign on invocation), function pinning (keep hot functions resident in memory), co-location with storage (functions run near their data). Stateful extension: functions can read/write durable state across invocations. Key insight: serverless can work for latency-sensitive workloads with pre-warming. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: URL Shortener (Bitly)
children.notes: Core: hash long URL to a short alphanumeric code (base62, 7 chars = 62^7 = 3.5T combinations). Storage: KV store mapping code to long URL. Redirect: HTTP 301 (permanent, browser caches, no analytics on repeat visits) vs 302 (temporary, every click hits server, enables analytics). Write path: generate unique code (hash function, or random + collision check), write to DB. Read path: lookup code, return redirect. Scale: very high read:write ratio (reads >> writes) -> heavy caching in Redis. Custom aliases: check availability, store directly. Analytics: log redirects to Kafka for downstream aggregation. Alex Xu Ch8.

children.name: Pastebin
children.notes: Store arbitrary text content with a short shareable URL. Architecture: content stored in object storage (S3), large, binary-safe; metadata (paste ID, creation time, expiry, author, visibility) in relational DB. Key generation: same approach as URL shortener (base62 random ID). TTL: option for pastes to auto-expire via S3 lifecycle rules or DB job. Scale: write-once read-many; CDN for popular public pastes. Similar to URL shortener for ID generation; the main difference is storing actual content vs a URL mapping. systemdesign42 website.

children.name: Distributed Counter
children.notes: Count events at high write throughput (likes, views, clicks, impressions). Naive: single DB counter (bottleneck at high QPS). Approaches: Redis INCR (atomic, single shard, bottleneck at ~100k/sec), sharded counters (maintain N counters, increment random shard, aggregate on read, write throughput scales to N*100k/sec but read is expensive), approximate counting with HyperLogLog for distinct counts. Consistency tradeoff: exact counts require coordination; approximate counts (~1% error) are often acceptable for engagement metrics. systemdesign42 website.

children.name: Real-Time Gaming Leaderboard
children.notes: Rank players by score globally or within a group. Redis Sorted Set (ZSET): ZADD O(log N), ZRANK O(log N), ZRANGE O(log N + M). Exact real-time global ranking for all players. For hyperscale: shard by game/region; each shard has a top-N leaderboard, aggregate top-N across shards for global leaderboard (approximate). Leaderboard windows: all-time, weekly, daily; use separate ZSETs per window and expire daily/weekly ones via TTL. Nearby ranks: ZRANGEBYSCORE around a player's score. systemdesign42 website.

children.name: Real-Time Live Comments
children.notes: Broadcast user comments to all viewers of a live stream simultaneously. WebSocket connections for low latency vs polling. Fan-out to O(N) concurrent viewers is the core challenge. Small scale: Redis Pub/Sub: comment published to channel, all subscribers receive via WebSocket. Large scale (millions): comments ingested to Kafka topic (partitioned by stream ID), consumer pool of WebSocket servers subscribe to relevant partition and push to connections. Rate limiting: prevent spam (N comments per second per user). Profanity filtering: async, publish and filter in parallel. systemdesign42 website.

children.name: Real-Time Presence Platform
children.notes: Know which users are online, offline, or idle. Heartbeat approach: client sends heartbeat every 5s; server records last_seen in Redis with TTL=15s. If TTL expires, user is offline. Scale: at 100M users * 5s = 20M heartbeats/sec. Shard presence by user_id consistent hash across presence servers. Change propagation: when a user's status changes, publish to Redis Pub/Sub or Kafka so all subscribers (friends, chat rooms) receive the update. Fan-out: user with 10k followers; push-on-write for small followings, pull-on-read for large followings. systemdesign42 website.

children.name: Virtual Waiting Room
children.notes: Metered access to high-demand resources (concert ticket sales, flash sales). Queue users behind a virtual room; grant access tokens as capacity opens. SeatGeek architecture: users enter queue via Redis sorted set (ZADD with timestamp as score = FIFO). Worker process pops from front, issues signed access tokens (JWT with expiry). Access token verified at checkout: expired or missing = back to queue. Anti-cheat: prevent users from holding multiple queue positions. Prevents site overload by controlling how many users are in the purchase flow simultaneously. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Design WhatsApp / Chat System
children.notes: Core components: real-time messaging (WebSocket for active users, push notifications for offline), message storage (Cassandra: write-heavy, time-ordered, 50B messages/day), message status (sent -> delivered -> read, three DB updates per message). Group messaging: fan-out when message received. Media: stored in object storage (S3), CDN for fast delivery. End-to-end encryption: Signal Protocol (Double Ratchet). WhatsApp: 50B messages/day with only 32 engineers using Erlang actor model where each conversation is a gen_server actor. Alex Xu Ch12, systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Design Slack
children.notes: Channel-based persistent messaging. WebSocket per client for real-time. Channel as pub/sub topic: messages published to channel, all channel members receive. Message storage: Cassandra (time-ordered, high write throughput). Search: Elasticsearch for message full-text search. Presence: heartbeat-based per user. File sharing: S3 + CDN, preview generation as async job. Compliance: message retention policies for regulated industries. Scale: Slack uses WebSocket load balancers that maintain connection state. At 10M+ DAU, fan-out for large channels is expensive: limit max channel size or use lazy fan-out. systemdesign42 website.

children.name: Design WeChat
children.notes: 1.67B monthly users. Super-app: IM + Moments (social feed) + Mini Programs + WeChat Pay. IM: MSYNC protocol for client-server sync (client pulls on reconnect). Moments: push-on-write for normal users, pull-on-read for celebrities. WeChat Pay: required distributed transactions for red packet (peer-to-peer payment to groups), all-or-nothing transfer under high concurrency. Multi-DC active-active replication. Mini Programs: sandboxed JS apps running inside WeChat client, an ecosystem of millions of apps without App Store distribution. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Design Zoom / Video Calls
children.notes: WebRTC for 1:1 calls: P2P, low latency, no media server needed. For group calls (3+): SFU (Selective Forwarding Unit): each participant uploads one stream, SFU selects and forwards relevant streams to each viewer. Alternatively MCU (Multipoint Control Unit): mixes all streams server-side into one composite; simpler for clients but high server CPU. Zoom: 300M daily meeting participants; audio goes over UDP (loss-tolerant), signaling over TCP/WebSocket. WebRTC TURN servers for NAT traversal. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Design YouTube / Video Streaming
children.notes: Upload pipeline: async transcoding into multiple resolutions (360p, 720p, 1080p, 4K) and codecs (H.264, AV1). Storage: raw video in S3, encoded chunks in S3, metadata in relational DB (MySQL at YouTube). CDN: encoded chunks served from edge. Adaptive bitrate streaming (HLS/DASH): client dynamically switches quality based on bandwidth. Recommendation: ML pipeline (collaborative filtering, watch history). Scale: YouTube 2.49B users on MySQL scaled via Vitess (sharding middleware). Alex Xu Ch14, systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Design Netflix
children.notes: CDN is the core product: Netflix Open Connect deploys custom appliances (OCAs) in ISP data centers and PoPs globally. 99% of traffic served from OCA edge cache. Microservices on AWS (~700 services). Chaos Engineering: Chaos Monkey, Chaos Kong (terminate entire region). Encoding: Netflix encodes each title hundreds of times at different bitrates and resolutions. Recommendation: offline collaborative filtering + real-time session features. Resiliency: every service assumes its dependencies will fail. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Design Instagram
children.notes: Scaled from 0 to 2.5B users. Storage: photos/videos in S3 + CDN. Metadata: PostgreSQL sharded via Citus. Hot data: Redis for follower lists and activity feeds. Feed generation: push-on-write for regular users (fan-out writes to follower feeds on post); pull-on-read for celebrities with millions of followers (too expensive to fan out). Hybrid: push for most users, pull + merge at read time for celebrity posts. Django web tier, Celery for async tasks. Key lesson: simple infrastructure (Postgres, Redis, S3) scaled further than expected before requiring distributed systems complexity. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Design Giphy / CDN
children.notes: Giphy: 10B GIFs/day to 1B users. Architecture is fundamentally a CDN problem: GIFs are large (1-5MB), access is global, top GIFs are very hot (Pareto distribution). Origin: GIFs stored in S3 or object storage. CDN: edge nodes cache popular GIFs geographically near users with long TTLs since GIFs are immutable. Search: Elasticsearch for GIF metadata (tags, source, trending score). API: lightweight metadata API returns CDN URLs, not the GIF bytes directly. CDN cache hit rate > 99% for popular content. Alex Xu Ch1 CDN section, systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Design Google Docs / Real-Time Collaboration
children.notes: Real-time sync challenge: concurrent edits from multiple users. Two approaches: Operational Transformation (OT): transform conflicting operations to preserve intent (Google Docs uses OT); CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types): merge-friendly data structures that converge without coordination (Figma, Notion use CRDTs). Architecture: WebSocket/RSocket for real-time transport; all operations sent to a central server which applies OT and broadcasts to all clients. Document state persisted in DB; operations logged for undo history. Canva: RSocket for 135M monthly users on real-time collaboration. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Design Spotify
children.notes: Music streaming with offline support. Tracks: stored as encrypted audio files in S3 + CDN, multiple bitrates (96kbps to 320kbps). Client caches tracks locally for offline mode. Discovery: collaborative filtering (matrix factorization, deep learning) + editorial playlists. Scale: personalized recommendations for 600M users. System design focus: CDN for low-latency audio delivery, search with Elasticsearch, recommendation pipeline (offline batch + online feature serving), content licensing DRM. systemdesign42 System Design Interview section.

children.name: Design Google Search / Web Crawler
children.notes: Crawler: BFS from seed URLs, URL frontier queue, politeness (robots.txt, crawl-delay, per-domain rate limits), duplicate detection (simhash for near-duplicate pages). Indexer: parse HTML, extract text and links, tokenize, build inverted index (term -> posting list of docID + TF). Ranking: PageRank (link graph authority) + TF-IDF + 200+ signals. Scale: ~100B web pages, PB of index. Distributed inverted index across thousands of machines. Query serving: query understanding (spell correction, entity recognition), index lookup, scoring, result serving. Alex Xu Ch9, systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Design Airbnb
children.notes: Search: Elasticsearch for listing search with geo queries (geohash or geo_distance filter), faceted filters (price, amenities, dates). Availability check: calendar model, availability table with listing_id + date + status. Booking: distributed transaction problem, reserve dates (optimistic locking, version column) and charge payment atomically. Pricing: dynamic pricing engine, per-night price rules. Messaging: host-guest messaging (chat system). Photos: S3 + CDN + async image processing. Review system: both parties review after stay. systemdesign42 System Design Interview section.

children.name: Design Twitter/X Timeline
children.notes: Fan-out problem: celebrity with 100M followers tweets. Approaches: push (write tweet to follower feeds at write time, fast reads, expensive writes), pull (read and merge followed users' tweets at read time, slow reads, cheap writes), hybrid (push for regular users with < N followers; pull + merge for celebrities). Implementation: Redis for timeline cache (sorted set by timestamp), Cassandra for tweet storage. QPS: 150k tweets/sec write, 600k timeline reads/sec. Alex Xu Ch11 (news feed). systemdesign42 System Design Interview section.

children.name: Design Reddit
children.notes: Community-based content voting and commenting. Posts: subreddit feed sorted by hot/top/new/controversial score. Upvote/downvote: atomic counter increment in Redis or DB. Hot ranking: Wilson score or Reddit's own algorithm (log(votes) + time decay). Comment threading: nested comments stored as adjacency list; rendered as trees. Real-time: WebSocket for live comment updates. Scale: media stored in S3, CDN for thumbnails. Search: Elasticsearch over posts and comments. Moderation: ML models + human mods. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Design ChatGPT
children.notes: Core: LLM inference API + chat history persistence. Components: chat session management (session ID to conversation history in DB or KV store), streaming responses (Server-Sent Events for token-by-token delivery), context window management (truncate/summarize history when approaching token limit), model serving (GPU clusters, load balanced, auto-scaling). Scale challenges: LLM inference is expensive (GPU compute), latency is inherently high (~1-5 tokens/sec), connection management for long SSE streams. RAG extension: vector DB for knowledge retrieval before LLM call. systemdesign42 System Design Interview section.

children.name: Design Uber / Ride-Sharing
children.notes: Geo-indexing: geohash (encode lat/lng to base32 string, nearby locations share prefix) or Google S2 cells for driver location queries. Matching: find nearest available drivers within radius via KD-tree or geospatial DB query. Dispatch: WebSocket to notify driver; push notification fallback. ETA: Dijkstra on road graph with live traffic weights; Uber computes 500k ETAs/sec. Surge pricing: supply/demand ratio per geohash cell triggers multiplier. Trip state machine: requested -> accepted -> picked up -> completed -> rated. Payments: idempotency keys prevent double charges. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Design a Payment System
children.notes: Core requirements: no double charges (idempotency), no lost payments (durability), regulatory compliance (PCI-DSS). Double-spend prevention: idempotency key per payment attempt (UUID from client); server checks if key was previously processed before executing. Atomicity: debit + credit in same DB transaction or via 2PC across services. Payment gateway integration: Stripe, Braintree, Adyen; async webhook callbacks for payment status. Reconciliation: nightly batch job compares internal state with bank settlement files and detects discrepancies. Uber: 30M transactions/day, ~400 transactions/sec. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Design Shopify / Flash Sales
children.notes: Flash sales create 100x+ normal traffic spikes. Rate limiting per store: token bucket to prevent one merchant's flash sale from affecting others. Inventory reservation: optimistic locking (version column on inventory row, retry on conflict) or Redis atomic DECR (DECR to 0, reject if negative). Cart and checkout: Redis for fast cart storage. CDN: cache product pages aggressively, invalidate on price/inventory changes. Shopify uses a modular monolith (not microservices) to minimize distributed systems complexity. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Design a Stock Exchange
children.notes: Matching engine: order book (bid + ask sides, sorted by price then time: price-time priority). Each symbol has its own order book. Matching engine is single-threaded to avoid concurrency complexity. LMAX Disruptor pattern: lock-free ring buffer (pre-allocated array), multiple producer single consumer, avoids GC pressure, achieves sub-microsecond latency. Market data: UDP multicast for broadcasts (best effort, clients reconstruct from sequence numbers + full snapshot). Order types: market, limit, stop, stop-limit. systemdesign42 newsletter three-part series.

children.name: Design Apple Pay
children.notes: NFC payment: device emulates a payment card. Tokenization: real card number replaced by a Device Account Number (DAN) stored in Secure Enclave. Each transaction: biometric auth (Face ID/Touch ID), generates a one-time dynamic security code (cryptogram). Payment network: DAN + cryptogram sent to Apple servers -> payment network (Visa/Mastercard) -> bank -> authorization. Privacy: merchant never sees real card number. Scale: 41M transactions/day. Secure Enclave: dedicated security chip, isolated from main processor. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Scaling to 10M Users on AWS
children.notes: Progressive architecture evolution from Alex Xu Ch1: (1) Single server (web + DB together). (2) Add DB server (separate web and data tiers). (3) Add load balancer + second web server (HA + horizontal scale). (4) Add DB read replicas (scale reads). (5) Add cache tier (Redis for hot data). (6) Add CDN (static assets). (7) Shard DB (scale writes). (8) Multiple data centers (global HA). Key AWS services: EC2, RDS (read replicas), ElastiCache, CloudFront, Route53 (geoDNS), S3. Each step driven by a specific bottleneck. Alex Xu Ch1, systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: How Facebook Scaled Live Video
children.notes: Live video is fundamentally different from on-demand (can't be precomputed or CDN-prefetched). Architecture: ingestion servers accept RTMP streams, transcode in real-time to multiple bitrates, push chunks to CDN edge servers. Fan-out: popular live streams (major sports events) can spike to millions of concurrent viewers in seconds. Edge caching: live chunks have short TTL (2-10 seconds) but cached at CDN edge. Adaptive bitrate: clients request appropriate quality based on network conditions. Flash crowds: reserve extra CDN capacity for scheduled events. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: How Cloudflare Handles 55M Requests/sec
children.notes: Anycast routing: every Cloudflare IP is announced from 300+ PoPs; traffic routed to nearest PoP. NGINX at edge: event-driven, handles 1M+ concurrent connections per instance. Only 15 Postgres clusters for all metadata despite 55M req/sec because 99%+ of requests are served entirely from edge cache without hitting origin or DB. PgBouncer for connection pooling to Postgres. Lua scripting in NGINX for edge logic. Key insight: the extreme cache hit rate means the database load is tiny relative to edge traffic. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: How Meta Achieves Cache Consistency
children.notes: Meta's TAO (The Associations and Objects) serves social graph data. 99.99999999% cache consistency. Problem: ensuring cache is up-to-date across regional data centers with millions of cache servers. Solution: lease-based invalidation (cache server issues a lease token when missing data, prevents thundering herd), hierarchical caching (local -> regional -> origin), read-after-write consistency via regional replication. Cache invalidation pipeline: DB write -> invalidation message on Memcache invalidation channel -> all relevant caches cleared. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Amazon Prime Video Microservices Failure
children.notes: 2023 blog post: Prime Video monitoring team initially built a microservices architecture on AWS Step Functions. At scale, costs were too high (~$17k/month for one feature). Root cause: Step Functions charged per state transition; monitoring pipeline made thousands of transitions per second per video stream. Solution: consolidated into a single monolith process, reducing costs by 90%. Key lesson: microservices are not always the right choice. Start with the simplest architecture; only distribute when you have a clear reason. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: How Figma Scaled Postgres to 4M Users
children.notes: Figma's multiplayer collaborative design required real-time DB writes for every cursor move and shape change. Started on a single Postgres instance. Scaling steps: vertical scaling (larger RDS), then PgBouncer for connection pooling (Postgres has ~100 connection limit without pooler), then read replicas for analytics queries, then partitioning hot tables by date. Avoided sharding for a long time by optimizing queries and caching aggressively in Redis. Key insight: Postgres scales further than people expect with proper pooling and indexing. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: How Dropbox Scaled to 100K Users
children.notes: Launched on Hacker News (2007), 75k signups in 24 hours. Initial architecture: Python/Django monolith, single MySQL DB, S3 for file storage. Scaling steps: (1) SQLite -> MySQL. (2) Added memcached for session and metadata caching. (3) Added read replicas. (4) Background job processing with Celery/RQ. (5) Split monolith into services (metadata service, block server). Key insight: Dropbox is fundamentally a file synchronization problem; block-level deduplication (Rsync-style deltas) reduced storage and bandwidth dramatically. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: How LinkedIn Scaled to 930M Users
children.notes: Pioneered many distributed systems patterns now commonplace. Key decisions: migrated from Oracle to Espresso (in-house NoSQL DB) and Voldemort (KV store). Kafka originated at LinkedIn for activity stream processing. Rest.li: internal REST framework. Caching: Proxy Cache then Redis. Protocol Buffers replaced JSON for service-to-service calls: 60% latency reduction. Helix: cluster management framework open-sourced from LinkedIn. Feed ranking: offline feature engineering + online serving via a scoring service. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: How Tinder Scaled to 1.6B Swipes per Day
children.notes: Core algorithm: show potential matches nearby. Geo-queries: PostGIS or Elasticsearch geo queries filtered by distance and preferences. Profile storage: Cassandra for user profiles (high write throughput). Photos: S3 + CDN. Matching: when both users swipe right, notification sent. Message: chat system on top of Kafka. Scaling swipes: 1.6B swipes/day = ~18.5k/sec. Redis for active user sessions and recommendation cache. Recommendation engine: collaborative filtering + preference model. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: How Discord Handles 30TB/day
children.notes: Discord stores messages in Cassandra (time-ordered, append-heavy, horizontally scalable). 30TB/day of message data stored. WebSocket connections for real-time: millions of concurrent connections across WebSocket servers. Voice/video: custom WebRTC infrastructure with regional media servers. Presence: custom presence system that scales to millions of simultaneous users via in-memory state + gossip. Read States: track last-read position per user per channel, extremely write-heavy at scale. Go backend for WebSocket handling (goroutines). systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: How Disney+ Scaled to 11M Users on Launch Day
children.notes: Disney+ launched November 2019 and immediately saw 10x projected traffic. Architecture: AWS-based, microservices. Buffering issues on day one traced to: concurrent session limits being too low (fixed within hours), CDN capacity exhaustion in some regions (CDN partners scaled rapidly). Key lesson: load test to 2x expected capacity; capacity plan for viral spikes. Progressive rollout: started with smaller markets (Netherlands) before US launch to discover issues. Graceful degradation: some features degraded rather than full outage. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: How to Approach System Design Interviews
children.notes: 4-step framework from Alex Xu Ch3: Step 1: Understand the problem and establish design scope (3-10 min): ask clarifying questions, define functional and non-functional requirements, agree on scope. Step 2: Propose high-level design and get buy-in (10-15 min): sketch components, validate with interviewer. Step 3: Design deep dive (10-25 min): go deep on 2-3 critical components. Step 4: Wrap up (3-5 min): discuss bottlenecks, improvements, edge cases. Communicate trade-offs at every step. Never jump into solutions before clarifying requirements. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Requirement Clarification
children.notes: Functional requirements: the specific capabilities the system must have. Non-functional requirements: scale (DAU, QPS, storage), latency (p99 target), availability (SLA), consistency (can we tolerate stale reads?), geographic distribution, security compliance. Questions to ask: Who are the users? What are the core features? How many users? Read or write heavy? What are the latency expectations? Do we need global distribution? Alex Xu Ch3: 'never jump into solutions without clarifying requirements, interviewers often intentionally leave problems vague.'

children.name: Capacity Estimation
children.notes: Back-of-the-envelope calculation to identify which components will be bottlenecks. Calculate: QPS (DAU * requests/user/day / 86400 * peak factor), storage (records/day * record size * retention * replication), bandwidth (QPS * payload size). Reference numbers: typical web server 1000 QPS, Redis ~100k QPS, Kafka 150k writes/sec/partition. These numbers drive architecture decisions: if writes > 100k QPS, you need DB sharding. If storage > 10TB/year, you need a distributed file system. Show calculations, don't just state results. Alex Xu Ch2.

children.name: High-Level Design
children.notes: Draw a block diagram showing major components and data flow between them. Standard components to consider: client -> DNS -> CDN (static) -> LB -> web servers -> cache (Redis) -> DB (primary/replica) -> message queue -> background workers -> object storage. Cover both read path and write path separately. Start simple (happy path, no failures), then discuss how to make it fault-tolerant and scalable. Get explicit buy-in from interviewer before diving deep. Alex Xu Ch3.

children.name: Deep Dive
children.notes: Spend the bulk of interview time here. Pick 2-3 components that are technically interesting or where the interviewer shows interest. For each: discuss data model/schema, failure modes, scalability approach, trade-offs considered. Common deep dive targets: database schema + indexing strategy, caching layer design (what to cache, eviction policy, invalidation), message queue + consumer design, API design + rate limiting, consistency model + replication strategy. Demonstrate that you know the trade-offs, not just the patterns. Alex Xu Ch3.

children.name: Common Failure Modes in Interviews
children.notes: 7 ways to fail from systemdesign42 newsletter: (1) No requirement clarification, jumping straight to solutions. (2) Over-engineering: proposing microservices and Kafka for a simple system. (3) Ignoring non-functional requirements: designing a system that works but can't scale. (4) Not discussing trade-offs: saying 'use Cassandra' without explaining why vs alternatives. (5) Designing in silence: not narrating your thought process. (6) Getting stuck on low-level details: spending all time on DB schema, missing high-level design. (7) Ignoring failure scenarios: not discussing what happens when components fail. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Thick vs Thin Client
children.notes: Thick client: app does most work on device (local data storage, business logic, background sync). Works offline; syncs to server when reconnected. Gmail, Notion, Spotify are thick clients. Thin client: app is a thin shell that sends all requests to server and renders responses; useless without connectivity. Tradeoff: thick = better UX offline, harder to update logic (must ship app update), harder to secure (logic on client). Thin = easy to update server logic, but requires reliable connectivity. Modern best practice: thick client for UX-critical paths (reading, composing), thin for sensitive operations (payments, auth, pricing). systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Push Notifications
children.notes: Server-to-client delivery of messages when the app is not in the foreground. Platform infrastructure: APNs (Apple Push Notification service) for iOS, FCM (Firebase Cloud Messaging) for Android. Flow: device registers with APNs/FCM and receives a device token; server sends notification payload to APNs/FCM with the device token; platform delivers to device. Token management: tokens change on app reinstall or OS update; server must handle token refresh via callback. Payload: limited to 4KB (APNs) or 4KB (FCM). Quiet/silent push: wake app in background for data sync without showing a notification. Alex Xu Ch10 (notification system design).

children.name: Request Batching & Payload Optimisation
children.notes: Reduce the number of network round-trips by combining multiple API calls into one request. GraphQL: a single query can fetch data across multiple resources that would require N REST calls. Batch API endpoints: POST /batch with array of sub-requests (used by Facebook Graph API). Payload optimisation: send only changed fields (PATCH vs PUT), use binary encoding (Protobuf vs JSON, ~3-5x smaller), gzip compression for HTTP bodies (Content-Encoding: gzip), image quality/resolution optimisation per device. On mobile: each additional network round-trip is expensive (3G RTT ~200ms, LTE ~50ms). systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Resumable Uploads
children.notes: For large file uploads (videos, large images) over unreliable mobile networks. Problem: a 100MB video upload interrupted at 99% must restart from zero with naive HTTP POST. Solution: chunk the file into parts (e.g., 5MB chunks); server tracks which chunks are received; on resume, client fetches the last received offset and continues from there. Protocols: TUS (open protocol), S3 Multipart Upload (each part acknowledged; CompleteMultipartUpload when done). AWS S3 Multipart: parts from 5MB, up to 10,000 parts, parallel upload of parts. Client resumes by listing uploaded parts (ListParts) and uploading missing ones. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Handling Intermittent Connectivity
children.notes: Mobile networks drop packets, switch between WiFi and cellular, and experience signal loss. Strategies: automatic retry with exponential backoff and jitter (prevents thundering herd on reconnect), offline queue (operations queued locally when offline, flushed on reconnect), optimistic updates (show success immediately, reconcile later), graceful degradation (show cached data with a 'you're offline' banner). iOS: NWPathMonitor for network status. Android: ConnectivityManager. Key principle: never show an error for temporary network loss — queue and retry transparently. DDIA Ch9 (unreliable networks) applied to mobile. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: On-Device Caching
children.notes: Cache API responses, images, and computed data locally on the device to reduce latency and support offline use. Types: in-memory cache (fast, lost on app kill, use for session data), disk cache (persistent, survives restarts, use for API responses and images). iOS: NSCache (in-memory), URLCache (HTTP response cache), Core Data / SQLite for structured data. Android: LruCache (in-memory), Room (SQLite ORM), OkHttp cache (HTTP). Image caching: Glide/Coil (Android), SDWebImage/Kingfisher (iOS) — disk + memory LRU cache for decoded bitmaps. Cache size limits: be conservative (50-100MB disk) since the OS can reclaim storage. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: HTTP Caching (ETag, Cache-Control)
children.notes: HTTP provides built-in caching that works without application logic. Cache-Control: max-age=N tells client how long to cache before re-fetching. ETag: server sends an opaque hash of the response (ETag: 'abc123'); client sends If-None-Match: 'abc123' on next request; server returns 304 Not Modified (no body) if unchanged, saving bandwidth. Last-Modified / If-Modified-Since: timestamp-based equivalent of ETag. Conditional GET: the combination of ETag or Last-Modified headers with conditional request headers. On mobile: conditional GETs are powerful since they save bandwidth even when the TTL expires. OkHttp (Android) automatically handles HTTP caching when a cache directory is configured. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Offline-First Architecture
children.notes: Design the app to work fully from local data, treating server sync as an optimistic enhancement rather than a requirement. Data stored locally (SQLite, Realm, Core Data, Room); UI reads from local store exclusively. Sync engine: background process that pushes local changes to server and pulls remote changes into local store. Conflict resolution required when same data is edited on multiple devices offline. Examples: Notion, Obsidian, Figma (partial offline), Linear, Superhuman. Challenges: merge conflicts, sync ordering, handling deletes (soft deletes), large initial data download. DDIA Ch12 (derived data, systems of record vs derived data). systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Local Database Design
children.notes: Choosing and structuring an on-device database. Options: SQLite (universal, relational, ACID; accessed via Room on Android, FMDB/Core Data on iOS), Realm (object database, reactive, fast, cross-platform), Core Data (iOS/macOS ORM over SQLite), IndexedDB (web/PWA). Key considerations: query patterns (design schema around queries, not normalization — mobile DBs rarely have complex joins), indices (add index on every frequently filtered column), WAL mode (Write-Ahead Log: enables concurrent reads during write, better performance). Avoid N+1 in local queries the same way you would server-side. Store user-generated content that must survive offline. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Data Modeling for Mobile Constraints
children.notes: Mobile devices have limited CPU, RAM, and battery. Data modeling decisions have outsized impact. Keep payloads small: return only fields the client displays (use sparse fieldsets or GraphQL). Pagination: cursor-based (infinite scroll) instead of offset. Flatten nested data: nested JSON requires deep traversal on a slow mobile CPU. Denormalize for read performance: store redundant data to avoid joins in local DB.
