Timestamp all records: required for delta sync and conflict resolution. Soft deletes: use deleted_at flag instead of hard delete so sync engines can propagate deletions. Use integer IDs where possible (smaller than UUIDs for storage and index efficiency). systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Delta Sync
children.notes: Instead of downloading the full dataset on every sync, download only what changed since the last sync. Server maintains a way to express changes: cursor/timestamp-based (return all records updated_at > last_sync_timestamp), sequence number (server assigns monotonically increasing seq to each change, client tracks last seen seq), changelog table (explicit log of all inserts/updates/deletes). Client sends last_sync_token; server returns only the delta. Benefits: dramatically reduces bandwidth and battery consumption. Challenges: deletions must be tracked explicitly (soft delete + include deleted_at in delta), large initial sync (full download on first use). Used by: Notion, Linear, Obsidian Sync. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Background Sync & Retry Queues
children.notes: Persist pending operations (writes, uploads) in a local queue so they survive app kills and network outages. Queue implementation: SQLite table with operation type, payload, status, retry_count, next_retry_at. Background sync: platform-specific job scheduler (Android: WorkManager with constraints=network available; iOS: BGTaskScheduler / BGProcessingTask). Retry policy: exponential backoff with jitter (retry after 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s... up to max). Idempotency: each queued operation must have a client-generated idempotency key so server-side retries are safe. Dead letter: after N failures, move to error queue for manual resolution or user notification. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Optimistic UI Updates
children.notes: Immediately update the UI as if the server operation succeeded, then reconcile once the server responds. Example: press Like — heart fills instantly, API call happens in background. If server succeeds: state is already correct. If server fails: revert the UI change and show an error. Benefits: perceived performance improvement (0ms UI response vs 300ms network RTT). Required state management: keep previous state to roll back. Conflict with background sync: if the server returns a different value (e.g., like count is higher than expected due to concurrent likes), merge the server response. Used by: Twitter likes, Instagram double-tap, Google Docs collaborative editing. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Eventual Consistency in Mobile Systems
children.notes: Mobile clients are inherently eventually consistent: a device offline for hours has stale data that must reconcile with the server on reconnect. Unlike server-side eventual consistency (seconds to minutes), mobile eventual consistency windows can be days. Key implications: never assume the server state matches local state; design UI to handle conflicts gracefully (show 'syncing...' indicators, handle server-returned corrections); use server-authoritative IDs (server assigns final ID after client creates with a temp local ID); version all data (updated_at or version counter) to detect staleness. Relates to DDIA Ch6 (eventual consistency) but the offline window and UI implications are mobile-specific. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Secure Token Storage
children.notes: Auth tokens (JWT access tokens, OAuth refresh tokens) must be stored securely to prevent theft by other apps or malware. iOS: Keychain (hardware-backed secure enclave storage, survives app reinstall if configured, access controlled by biometrics). Android: Keystore (hardware-backed, keys never leave secure hardware; EncryptedSharedPreferences for token storage). Never store tokens in: plain SharedPreferences/NSUserDefaults (readable by other processes on rooted devices), local SQLite without encryption (readable on rooted devices), logs. Token rotation: use short-lived access tokens (15 min) + long-lived refresh tokens; rotate refresh tokens on use (refresh token rotation). systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Certificate Pinning
children.notes: Prevents man-in-the-middle attacks by hardcoding the expected server TLS certificate or public key in the app. On TLS handshake: app compares server's certificate against the pinned value; if they don't match, connection is rejected — even if the certificate is signed by a trusted CA. Variants: certificate pinning (pin the full cert, brittle: must update app on cert renewal), public key pinning (pin the public key, survives cert renewal if same key is used). iOS: NSURLSession with custom URLSessionDelegate. Android: OkHttp CertificatePinner. Risk: if pinned cert expires and app isn't updated, app breaks. Backup pins: always include a backup pin for certificate rotation. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Biometric Authentication
children.notes: Use fingerprint (Touch ID) or face recognition (Face ID, Android BiometricPrompt) to authenticate the user or unlock sensitive app sections without requiring a password re-entry. Implementation: OS handles biometric verification; on success, the OS releases a Keychain/Keystore key that decrypts the local credentials or signs an auth assertion. App never sees the raw biometric data (handled entirely by secure enclave). Fallback: always offer PIN/password fallback for when biometrics fail or are unavailable. Use cases: unlock app after background, authorize payments, decrypt locally stored data. Not a replacement for server-side auth: biometrics only prove physical presence at the device. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Code Obfuscation & Tamper Detection
children.notes: Code obfuscation: rename classes, methods, and variables to meaningless names to make reverse engineering harder. iOS: Swift code is partially obfuscated by default but Objective-C is not; tools like iXGuard/Obfuscator-LLVM. Android: ProGuard/R8 (bundled with Android Gradle plugin) renames and removes dead code; DexGuard for stronger obfuscation. Tamper detection: verify app signature at runtime (Android PackageManager.getPackageInfo with GET_SIGNATURES); detect if app is running in a debugger or emulator (Android Debug.isDebuggerConnected, BuildConfig.DEBUG). Root/jailbreak detection: check for su binary, Cydia, known root apps. Obfuscation slows reverse engineering but doesn't stop a determined attacker. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Secure Deep Linking
children.notes: Deep links open specific app screens from URLs (e.g., myapp://product/123 or https://myapp.com/product/123). Security risks: another app can register the same URL scheme and intercept links (URL scheme hijacking). Universal Links (iOS) / App Links (Android): HTTPS-based deep links validated by a server-hosted file (apple-app-site-association / assetlinks.json) that proves the app owns the domain. This prevents other apps from hijacking the link. Never trust deep link parameters without validation: always sanitize and authorize; a malicious link could pass crafted parameters to bypass in-app auth checks. Deferred deep links: route new users through install then to the intended screen. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: User Data Privacy
children.notes: Regulations: GDPR (EU — right to erasure, data portability, consent required), CCPA (California — opt-out of data sale), COPPA (US — no data collection from under-13s). Principles: data minimization (collect only what you use), purpose limitation (don't use data beyond stated purpose), retention limits (delete data after N days). Technical measures: anonymization (remove PII before analytics), pseudonymization (replace PII with tokens), differential privacy (add mathematical noise to aggregate queries — used by Apple for usage statistics). App Store requirements: Privacy Nutrition Labels (iOS) and Data Safety section (Android) require declaring what data is collected. DDIA Ch14 (ethics and privacy) is the foundational reference. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: App Lifecycle & State Management
children.notes: iOS app states: Not Running, Inactive, Active, Background (limited time), Suspended (frozen in memory, can be killed without warning). Android: Activity/Fragment lifecycle (onCreate, onStart, onResume, onPause, onStop, onDestroy) + process lifecycle (foreground service, background restrictions in Android 8+). Critical: save state before app goes to background (iOS: applicationWillResignActive; Android: onSaveInstanceState) so it can be restored after kill. Memory pressure: iOS sends memory warnings (applicationDidReceiveMemoryWarning); Android trims memory callbacks (onTrimMemory). Background work limits: iOS background time is ~30 seconds after going to background. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Background Processing Limits
children.notes: OS platforms aggressively restrict background processing to save battery. iOS: apps get ~30s background execution after going to background (beginBackgroundTask); BGAppRefreshTask for periodic fetch (min ~15min interval, OS decides actual timing); BGProcessingTask for long tasks (only when plugged in). Android: Doze mode (introduced Android 6) defers network and CPU in standby; App Standby Buckets reduce background job frequency for rarely-used apps; WorkManager: the recommended API that handles all background scheduling constraints (network, charging, idle). Key rule: never assume background tasks run promptly; design for eventual execution. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Battery Optimisation
children.notes: Battery drain sources: GPS/location (highest drain), Bluetooth/WiFi scanning, CPU-intensive work, screen wake (WakeLocks), frequent network requests. Strategies: batch network requests (reduce radio wake-ups; mobile radio takes ~2s to transition from idle to active state), use push over polling (FCM/APNs wakes app only when data arrives vs polling every N seconds), exponential backoff for retries, use passive location (network/WiFi instead of GPS), avoid WakeLocks, release resources (camera, microphone, sensors) in onPause/onStop. iOS: Instruments Energy Log; Android: Android Battery Historian for diagnosing drain. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Startup Time Optimisation
children.notes: App startup types: cold start (process not in memory, slowest), warm start (process in memory but activity destroyed), hot start (activity in memory, fastest). Cold start time target: < 500ms (Google recommends < 2s, Apple recommends < 400ms for initial display). Optimizations: defer non-critical initializations to post-launch (lazy init, background threads), avoid synchronous disk I/O on main thread at startup, use App Startup Library (Android) to sequence init in the correct order, reduce number of libraries with heavy startup costs, profile with Android Profiler or Xcode Instruments Time Profiler, use baseline profiles (Android) to pre-JIT critical code paths. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Rendering Performance
children.notes: Smooth 60 FPS (16ms per frame) or 120 FPS (8ms per frame) on ProMotion displays. Main thread rule: all UI updates must happen on the main thread; blocking the main thread causes dropped frames (jank). Common causes of jank: synchronous disk/network I/O on main thread, expensive layout passes (deeply nested view hierarchies), overdraw (multiple layers painting same pixel). Optimizations: RecyclerView/UICollectionView for lists (only render visible cells, recycle off-screen), flatten view hierarchies (ConstraintLayout on Android, avoid deep SwiftUI view trees), prefetch and cache decoded images (avoid decoding on main thread), use hardware-accelerated layers. Profile with Xcode Instruments Core Animation or Android GPU Rendering. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: Memory Management & Leak Prevention
children.notes: Mobile devices have limited RAM (2-8GB on mid-range phones). OOM (Out Of Memory) kills are silent: Android kills background processes; iOS terminates apps that use too much memory. Common leaks: retained strong references to Activity/ViewController in callbacks (use weak self in Swift/Kotlin closures), static references to Context (Android), large bitmap allocations not recycled. Detection tools: Android Memory Profiler (heap dumps, allocation tracking), Xcode Memory Graph Debugger, LeakCanary (Android library that detects leaks at runtime in debug builds). Image memory: a 4K image is 48MB decoded in memory; always downsample to display size before caching. ARC (iOS) and GC (Android) handle allocation but not reference cycles. systemdesign42 newsletter.

children.name: App Size Optimisation
children.notes: Smaller apps download faster, get more installs (every 6MB reduction = ~1% install increase per Google study). Techniques: Android App Bundle (AAB) — Google Play strips unused density/ABI/language resources per device, reducing delivery size by ~20-30%. On-demand feature modules: download large features (AR, advanced editor) only when first used. Image compression: WebP format (25-34% smaller than JPEG at same quality), vector drawables for icons instead of multiple PNG densities. Code shrinking: R8/ProGuard removes unused code and resources. Avoid bundling large libraries when only a small portion is used (check method count on Android). iOS App Thinning: App Slicing + On-Demand Resources + Bitcode. systemdesign42 newsletter.
